0: <title>Life Cycle Assessment of Fertilization of Corn and Corn-Soybean Rotations with Swine Manure and Synthetic Fertilizer in lowa </title>
Evan Michael Griffing, Richard Lynn Schauer, and Charles W. Rice*

Life cycle assessment is the predominant method to compare energy and environmental impacts of agricultural production systems. In this life cycle study, we focused on the comparison of swine manure to synthetic fertilizer as nutrients for corn production in Iowa. Deep pit (DP) and anaerobic lagoon (AL) treatment systems were compared separately, and urea ammonium nitrate (UAN) was chosen as the representative synthetic fertilizer. The two functional units used were fertilization of 1000 kg of corn in a continuous corn system and fertilization of a crop yielding 1000 kg of corn and a crop yielding 298 kg of soybean in a 2-yr corn-soybean rotation. Iowa- specific versions of emission factors and energy use were used when available and compared with Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change values. Manure was lower than synthetic fertilizer for abiotic depletion and about equal with respect to eutrophication. Synthetic fertilizer was lower than manure for global warming potential (GWP) and acidification. The choice of allocation method and life cycle boundary were important in understanding the context of these results. In the DP system, methane (CH4) from housing was the largest contributor to the GWP, accounting for 60% of the total impact. When storage systems were compared, the DP system had 50% less GWP than the AL system. This comparison was due to reduction in CH4 emissions from the storage system and conservation of nitrogen. Nitrous oxide emissions were the biggest contributor to the GWP of UAN fertilization and the second biggest contributor to the GWP of manure. Monte Carlo and scenario analyses were used to test the robustness of the results and sensitivity to methodology and important impact factors. The available crop-land and associated plant nutrient needs in Iowa was compared with manure production for the current hog population. On a state- or county-wide level, there was generally an excess of available land. On a farm level, there is often an excess of manure, which necessitates long-distance transport.

W ITH INCREASING ENERGY and environmen- tal awareness by society, evaluating the energy and environmental impacts of agricultural production systems is needed to compare system components. Life cycle assessment (LCA) has been used to compare energy and envi- ronmental impacts of agricultural production systems (Charles et al., 2006; Beauchemin et al., 2010; Ogino et al., 2012; O'Brien et al., 2012).

In 2012, global production of swine was 104 million metric tons, an increase of about 40% since 1997. Of this, 10.4 million metric tons was produced in the United States (USDA, 2013). With an expanding population and an increasing desire for protein, large quantities of manure from livestock such as swine will continue to be produced in the future. Properly handled, manure can be a valuable resource. Without proper management, emissions of the greenhouse gases (GHGs) methane (CH4) and nitrous oxide (N,O) from swine manure become an environmental threat. Swine manure represents about 25% of a farm gate-to-gate total global warming potential (GWP) (Thoma et al., 2011). Information about the origin, handling, and dispensation of manure is needed for proper management of manure as a resource and to minimize its potential environmental impact on global climate change and water quality. An LCA comparing swine manure with synthetic fertilizer elucidates the benefits and concerns of using manure as a resource.
J. Environ. Qual. 43:709-722 (2014) doi: 10.2134/jeq2013.04.0112 Received 2 Apr. 2013. *Corresponding author (cwrice@ksu.edu).

Reckman et al. (2012) provides an overview of LCA and the main environmental issues and impacts of pork production. Global warming potential, acidification, eutrophication, and primary resource use are important for manure management in particular and for pork production in general. Lopez-Ridaura et al. (2009) and Griffing et al. (2004) used LCA to compare alternative methods of swine waste disposal. Both research groups found that control of nitrogen (N) (as ammonia [NH3] and N,O), CH4 emissions, and conservation of N as a resource were important drivers in the assessment. Stone et al. (2012) compared the LCA results of pork production using several

Schauer, Schauer Associates, PO Box 398, Townsend, DE 19734; C.W. Rice, Kansas State Univ., Dep. of Agronomy, 2004 Throckmorton Plant Science Center, Kansas State Univ., Manhattan, KS 66506. Abbreviations: AL, anaerobic lagoon; BMP, best management practices; DNR, (lowa) Department of Natural Resources; DP, deep pit; GHG, greenhouse gas; GWP, global warming potential; IPCC, Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change; LCA, life cycle assessment; LGN, lognormal distribution; MRTN, maximum return to nitrogen; MWPS, Midwest Plan Service; S, synthetic; SD2, square of geometric standard deviation; TSP. triple superphosphate; TVS, total volatile solids; UAN, urea ammonium nitrate; VS, volatile solids.

alternative feeds in the Northern Great Plains region of the United States. Using a diet of corn and soymeal, they showed very similar results for fertilization with synthetic fertilizers and a mix of 40% manure and 60% synthetic fertilizers.

Swine manure from a three slurry systems was compared with synthetic fertilizers as a nutrient for production of fruit using an ecological footprint analysis by Cerutti et al. (2011). The authors discuss the issue of allocating manure emissions to the animal production or food production system. This is a typical example of open-loop recycling and is also discussed in relation to pork production by Basset-Mens and van der Werf (2005). Cerutti et al. (2011) found that the ecological footprint was smaller (lower impact) for synthetic fertilization than for manure. The quality and transparency of life cycle data are critical in any LCA study. This holds true for swine production (Basset-Mens et al., 2007). There is a trade-off between using generic data that have been widely vetted and location and/or management practice specific data, which may be more appropriate but may have a larger uncertainty (IPCC, 2006; Basset-Mens et al., 2006).

Global climate change is an important environmental issue as a consequence of the increasing emissions of GHGs, such as N2O, CH4 and carbon dioxide (CO2), from anthropogenic activities (Chang Seng, 2007). Whereas increases in atmospheric CO, concentrations are due primarily to fossil fuel use, increases in atmospheric CH4 are attributed to agriculture and industrial activities (IPCC, 2007). Increases in N2O are principally (85%) from agricultural activities (IPCC, 2007) as a result of N enrichment of agriculturally managed soils. Globally, agriculture activities contributed 13.5% of the anthropogenic GHG emissions in 2004 (IPCC, 2007). Although synthetic fertilizers and animal manures are significant sources of GHGs, their application to cropped soils is required to provide nutrient needs to meet global food demand (Mosier et al., 1998) and to convert a waste product into a resource.

Agricultural soils amended with manure or synthetic N fertilizers are known to increase N2O emissions because of enhanced nitrification and denitrification processes. This has been recognized as an important contributor to the GWP of agricultural products. In addition, the amount of these emissions is the source of significant uncertainty and controversy in the LCA (Smith and Searchinger, 2012; Crutzen et al., 2008). Of particular importance in this LCA is the comparison of emissions from synthetic fertilized and manured fields. Several studies have reported greater N2O emissions after manure application compared with synthetic fertilizer N application (Rochette al., 2008; Velthof et al., 2003; Chantigny et al., 2010); conversely, other studies have reported lower emissions (Chantigny et al., 2010; Sanchez-Martin et al., 2010; Yao et al., 2010).

Many strategies, primarily timing and rate of application, have been studied to examine their effectiveness for reducing N2O emissions. The nonlinear pattern between N application rate and emissions suggests that N rates above the economic optimum dramatically increase N2O emissions (McSwiney and Robertson, 2005; Millar et al., 2010; Hoben et al., 2011). Excessive or mistimed application of manure, however, leads to increased emissions of GHG and eutrophication of watersheds by nitrates and phosphates.
In this life cycle study, swine manure from deep pit (DP) and anaerobic lagoon (AL) storage systems was compared with

synthetic fertilizer as a nutrient for corn and soybean production in Iowa. Comparisons were made on the basis of corn/corn (CC) rotation and corn/soybean (CS) rotation. Open-loop recycling was handled in a similar manner as in Cerutti et al. (2011). Region- and management-specific assumptions were made regarding emission factors, and the impact of this on uncertainty was addressed through Monte Carlo and scenario analyses.

Swine manure from a three slurry systems was compared with synthetic fertilizers as a nutrient for production of fruit using an ecological footprint analysis by Cerutti et al. (2011). The authors discuss the issue of allocating manure emissions to the animal production or food production system. This is a typical example of open-loop recycling and is also discussed in relation to pork production by Basset-Mens and van der Werf (2005). Cerutti et al. (2011) found that the ecological footprint was smaller (lower impact) for synthetic fertilization than for manure. The quality and transparency of life cycle data are critical in any LCA study. This holds true for swine production (Basset-Mens et al., 2007). There is a trade-off between using generic data that have been widely vetted and location and/or management practice specific data, which may be more appropriate but may have a larger uncertainty (IPCC, 2006; Basset-Mens et al., 2006).

Global climate change is an important environmental issue as a consequence of the increasing emissions of GHGs, such as N2O, CH4 and carbon dioxide (CO2), from anthropogenic activities (Chang Seng, 2007). Whereas increases in atmospheric CO, concentrations are due primarily to fossil fuel use, increases in atmospheric CH4 are attributed to agriculture and industrial activities (IPCC, 2007). Increases in N2O are principally (85%) from agricultural activities (IPCC, 2007) as a result of N enrichment of agriculturally managed soils. Globally, agriculture activities contributed 13.5% of the anthropogenic GHG emissions in 2004 (IPCC, 2007). Although synthetic fertilizers and animal manures are significant sources of GHGs, their application to cropped soils is required to provide nutrient needs to meet global food demand (Mosier et al., 1998) and to convert a waste product into a resource.

Agricultural soils amended with manure or synthetic N fertilizers are known to increase N2O emissions because of enhanced nitrification and denitrification processes. This has been recognized as an important contributor to the GWP of agricultural products. In addition, the amount of these emissions is the source of significant uncertainty and controversy in the LCA (Smith and Searchinger, 2012; Crutzen et al., 2008). Of particular importance in this LCA is the comparison of emissions from synthetic fertilized and manured fields. Several studies have reported greater N2O emissions after manure application compared with synthetic fertilizer N application (Rochette al., 2008; Velthof et al., 2003; Chantigny et al., 2010); conversely, other studies have reported lower emissions (Chantigny et al., 2010; Sanchez-Martin et al., 2010; Yao et al., 2010).

Many strategies, primarily timing and rate of application, have been studied to examine their effectiveness for reducing N2O emissions. The nonlinear pattern between N application rate and emissions suggests that N rates above the economic optimum dramatically increase N2O emissions (McSwiney and Robertson, 2005; Millar et al., 2010; Hoben et al., 2011). Excessive or mistimed application of manure, however, leads to increased emissions of GHG and eutrophication of watersheds by nitrates and phosphates.
In this life cycle study, swine manure from deep pit (DP) and anaerobic lagoon (AL) storage systems was compared with

synthetic fertilizer as a nutrient for corn and soybean production in Iowa. Comparisons were made on the basis of corn/corn (CC) rotation and corn/soybean (CS) rotation. Open-loop recycling was handled in a similar manner as in Cerutti et al. (2011). Region- and management-specific assumptions were made regarding emission factors, and the impact of this on uncertainty was addressed through Monte Carlo and scenario analyses.
4: <title>Life Cycle Assessment of Fertilization of Corn and Corn-Soybean Rotations with Swine Manure and Synthetic Fertilizer in lowa </title>
Evan Michael Griffing, Richard Lynn Schauer, and Charles W. Rice*

Life cycle assessment is the predominant method to compare energy and environmental impacts of agricultural production systems. In this life cycle study, we focused on the comparison of swine manure to synthetic fertilizer as nutrients for corn production in Iowa. Deep pit (DP) and anaerobic lagoon (AL) treatment systems were compared separately, and urea ammonium nitrate (UAN) was chosen as the representative synthetic fertilizer. The two functional units used were fertilization of 1000 kg of corn in a continuous corn system and fertilization of a crop yielding 1000 kg of corn and a crop yielding 298 kg of soybean in a 2-yr corn-soybean rotation. Iowa- specific versions of emission factors and energy use were used when available and compared with Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change values. Manure was lower than synthetic fertilizer for abiotic depletion and about equal with respect to eutrophication. Synthetic fertilizer was lower than manure for global warming potential (GWP) and acidification. The choice of allocation method and life cycle boundary were important in understanding the context of these results. In the DP system, methane (CH4) from housing was the largest contributor to the GWP, accounting for 60% of the total impact. When storage systems were compared, the DP system had 50% less GWP than the AL system. This comparison was due to reduction in CH4 emissions from the storage system and conservation of nitrogen. Nitrous oxide emissions were the biggest contributor to the GWP of UAN fertilization and the second biggest contributor to the GWP of manure. Monte Carlo and scenario analyses were used to test the robustness of the results and sensitivity to methodology and important impact factors. The available crop-land and associated plant nutrient needs in Iowa was compared with manure production for the current hog population. On a state- or county-wide level, there was generally an excess of available land. On a farm level, there is often an excess of manure, which necessitates long-distance transport.

Monte Carlo and scenario analyses were used to test the robustness of the results and sensitivity to methodology and important impact factors. The available crop-land and associated plant nutrient needs in Iowa was compared with manure production for the current hog population. On a state- or county-wide level, there was generally an excess of available land. On a farm level, there is often an excess of manure, which necessitates long-distance transport.

Properly handled, manure can be a valuable resource. Without proper management, emissions of the greenhouse gases (GHGs) methane (CH4) and nitrous oxide (N,O) from swine manure become an environmental threat. Swine manure represents about 25% of a farm gate-to-gate total global warming potential (GWP) (Thoma et al., 2011). Information about the origin, handling, and dispensation of manure is needed for proper management of manure as a resource and to minimize its potential environmental impact on global climate change and water quality. Abbreviations: AL, anaerobic lagoon; BMP, best management practices; DNR, (lowa) Department of Natural Resources; DP, deep pit; GHG, greenhouse gas; GWP, global warming potential; IPCC, Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change; LCA, life cycle assessment; LGN, lognormal distribution; MRTN, maximum return to nitrogen; MWPS, Midwest Plan Service; S, synthetic; SD2, square of geometric standard deviation; TSP. triple superphosphate; TVS, total volatile solids; UAN, urea ammonium nitrate; VS, volatile solids.

alternative feeds in the Northern Great Plains region of the United States. Using a diet of corn and soymeal, they showed very similar results for fertilization with synthetic fertilizers and a mix of 40% manure and 60% synthetic fertilizers.

Swine manure from a three slurry systems was compared with synthetic fertilizers as a nutrient for production of fruit using an ecological footprint analysis by Cerutti et al. (2011). The authors discuss the issue of allocating manure emissions to the animal production or food production system. This is a typical example of open-loop recycling and is also discussed in relation to pork production by Basset-Mens and van der Werf (2005). Cerutti et al. (2011) found that the ecological footprint was smaller (lower impact) for synthetic fertilization than for manure. The quality and transparency of life cycle data are critical in any LCA study. This holds true for swine production (Basset-Mens et al., 2007). There is a trade-off between using generic data that have been widely vetted and location and/or management practice specific data, which may be more appropriate but may have a larger uncertainty (IPCC, 2006; Basset-Mens et al., 2006).

Global climate change is an important environmental issue as a consequence of the increasing emissions of GHGs, such as N2O, CH4 and carbon dioxide (CO2), from anthropogenic activities (Chang Seng, 2007). Whereas increases in atmospheric CO, concentrations are due primarily to fossil fuel use, increases in atmospheric CH4 are attributed to agriculture and industrial activities (IPCC, 2007). Increases in N2O are principally (85%) from agricultural activities (IPCC, 2007) as a result of N enrichment of agriculturally managed soils. Globally, agriculture activities contributed 13.5% of the anthropogenic GHG emissions in 2004 (IPCC, 2007). Although synthetic fertilizers and animal manures are significant sources of GHGs, their application to cropped soils is required to provide nutrient needs to meet global food demand (Mosier et al., 1998) and to convert a waste product into a resource.

Agricultural soils amended with manure or synthetic N fertilizers are known to increase N2O emissions because of enhanced nitrification and denitrification processes. This has been recognized as an important contributor to the GWP of agricultural products. In addition, the amount of these emissions is the source of significant uncertainty and controversy in the LCA (Smith and Searchinger, 2012; Crutzen et al., 2008). Of particular importance in this LCA is the comparison of emissions from synthetic fertilized and manured fields. Several studies have reported greater N2O emissions after manure application compared with synthetic fertilizer N application (Rochette al., 2008; Velthof et al., 2003; Chantigny et al., 2010); conversely, other studies have reported lower emissions (Chantigny et al., 2010; Sanchez-Martin et al., 2010; Yao et al., 2010).

Many strategies, primarily timing and rate of application, have been studied to examine their effectiveness for reducing N2O emissions. The nonlinear pattern between N application rate and emissions suggests that N rates above the economic optimum dramatically increase N2O emissions (McSwiney and Robertson, 2005; Millar et al., 2010; Hoben et al., 2011). Excessive or mistimed application of manure, however, leads to increased emissions of GHG and eutrophication of watersheds by nitrates and phosphates.
In this life cycle study, swine manure from deep pit (DP) and anaerobic lagoon (AL) storage systems was compared with

synthetic fertilizer as a nutrient for corn and soybean production in Iowa. Comparisons were made on the basis of corn/corn (CC) rotation and corn/soybean (CS) rotation. Open-loop recycling was handled in a similar manner as in Cerutti et al. (2011). Region- and management-specific assumptions were made regarding emission factors, and the impact of this on uncertainty was addressed through Monte Carlo and scenario analyses.
8: <title>Life Cycle Assessment of Fertilization of Corn and Corn-Soybean Rotations with Swine Manure and Synthetic Fertilizer in lowa </title>
Evan Michael Griffing, Richard Lynn Schauer, and Charles W. Rice*

Life cycle assessment is the predominant method to compare energy and environmental impacts of agricultural production systems. In this life cycle study, we focused on the comparison of swine manure to synthetic fertilizer as nutrients for corn production in Iowa. Deep pit (DP) and anaerobic lagoon (AL) treatment systems were compared separately, and urea ammonium nitrate (UAN) was chosen as the representative synthetic fertilizer. The two functional units used were fertilization of 1000 kg of corn in a continuous corn system and fertilization of a crop yielding 1000 kg of corn and a crop yielding 298 kg of soybean in a 2-yr corn-soybean rotation. Iowa- specific versions of emission factors and energy use were used when available and compared with Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change values. Manure was lower than synthetic fertilizer for abiotic depletion and about equal with respect to eutrophication. Synthetic fertilizer was lower than manure for global warming potential (GWP) and acidification. The choice of allocation method and life cycle boundary were important in understanding the context of these results. In the DP system, methane (CH4) from housing was the largest contributor to the GWP, accounting for 60% of the total impact. When storage systems were compared, the DP system had 50% less GWP than the AL system. This comparison was due to reduction in CH4 emissions from the storage system and conservation of nitrogen. Nitrous oxide emissions were the biggest contributor to the GWP of UAN fertilization and the second biggest contributor to the GWP of manure. Monte Carlo and scenario analyses were used to test the robustness of the results and sensitivity to methodology and important impact factors. The available crop-land and associated plant nutrient needs in Iowa was compared with manure production for the current hog population. On a state- or county-wide level, there was generally an excess of available land. On a farm level, there is often an excess of manure, which necessitates long-distance transport.

10: <header>Methods and Inventory Data Farm Practice and Prevailing Legislative Rules </header>

From 1998 to 2009, the trend in Iowa was an increase in the number of hogs produced coincident with a decrease in the number of farms. Confined animal feeding operations with more than 1000 animal units along with increased fertilizer prices, stricter and more rigorous enforcement of regulations, and programs such as United States Department of Agriculture Natural Resource Conservation Service's Environmental Quality Incentives Program have stimulated an increased share of the total cropland receiving manure, increased incorporation of manure immediately after application, an increased share of farms removing manure from their farming operations, increased testing of manure nutrient contents, and increased use of phytase in feeds to reduce phosphorus (P) content of manure (Key et al., 2011). The larger confined animal feeding operations often lack sufficient crop acreage to use the manure generated, which leads to a greater percentage of manure transported off-farm. This transport adds to economic and environmental costs.

In Iowa, the Department of Natural Resources (DNR) regulates manure handling, stipulating that all manure must be used within 1 yr and placing limits on application amounts. The P-index, an estimation of risk for P losses to the environment, is described by the Iowa DNR (IA DNR, 2013a). Rules governing application on a P basis or N basis are described in Iowa DNR 542-4000a (IA DNR 2013b). When the risk of P losses is low, these limits are expressed on an N basis of 21.4 Nkg- corn. When risk is higher due to a combination of soil P build-up, landscape features, soil type, and proximity to streams, application limits are expressed on a P basis of 6.7 g P kg-1 corn. In high-risk regions, manure cannot be applied. For soybean, application limits for N and P are 63.3 g N kg-1 soybean and 13.2 g P 6 kg soybean. Soil analyses must be performed every 2 yr, which, along with nutrient analyses of the manure, will govern the amount of manure to be applied. A residual N credit of 56.0 kg N ha is factored into applications on corn following soybean. One recommended practice in Iowa (i.e., "rule of thumb") is to apply N at a rate of 17.9 kg corn, which is less than the mandated upper limit but more favorable in terms of economics (Sawyer et al., 2006). In a commonly used rotation, fields receive manure fertilization on corn the first year, no fertilization on soybean the second year, synthetic N based on soil tests on corn the third year, and no fertilizer on soybean the fourth year.

12: Manure nutrient excretion rates have been specified by the Midwest Plan Service (MWPS) (Lorimor et al., 2004), by the American Society of Agricultural Engineers (ASAE, 2005), and by Barker and Overcash (2007). In this study, the MWPS data, which are representative of the midwestern United States and

Iowa, were used (Table 1). Manure excretion rates per kilogram of live weight (LW) were higher for nursing hogs and were mostly constant throughout the finishing weights. To obtain a representative values over the animal life, total N, P, and K excretion rates were integrated over a typical growth profile to determine time-weighted average rates. The MWPS and American Society of Agricultural Engineers manure nutrient characteristics are similar. The Barker and Overcash (2007) data, which were direct measurements mostly collected between 1965 and 1984, show higher P content (0.14 g P kg LW-1 d-1). Reductions in P content over time have been attributed to improvements in diet (Lorimor et al., 2004) and possibly to differences in measurement methods (Barker and Overcash, 2007).

The amount of N to achieve the optimum return on investment, called the maximum return to N (MRTN), is 17.3 N kg-1 corn (Sawyer et al., 2006). A rule of thumb application rate used by farmers in the region is 17.9 g N kg corn, which is close to the calculated optimum. In addition to economic savings, using the MRTN results in less environmental impact (Sawyer et al., 2006; Nafziger et al., 2004). Any of these approaches (P-normalized, rule of thumb, or MRTN) significantly lowers the amount of fertilizer applied per acre with subsequently less NO3 and phosphate losses into drainage systems.

In this study, the MWPS data, which are representative of the midwestern United States and

Iowa, were used (Table 1). Manure excretion rates per kilogram of live weight (LW) were higher for nursing hogs and were mostly constant throughout the finishing weights. To obtain a representative values over the animal life, total N, P, and K excretion rates were integrated over a typical growth profile to determine time-weighted average rates. The MWPS and American Society of Agricultural Engineers manure nutrient characteristics are similar. The Barker and Overcash (2007) data, which were direct measurements mostly collected between 1965 and 1984, show higher P content (0.14 g P kg LW-1 d-1). Reductions in P content over time have been attributed to improvements in diet (Lorimor et al., 2004) and possibly to differences in measurement methods (Barker and Overcash, 2007).

The amount of N to achieve the optimum return on investment, called the maximum return to N (MRTN), is 17.3 N kg-1 corn (Sawyer et al., 2006). A rule of thumb application rate used by farmers in the region is 17.9 g N kg corn, which is close to the calculated optimum. In addition to economic savings, using the MRTN results in less environmental impact (Sawyer et al., 2006; Nafziger et al., 2004). Any of these approaches (P-normalized, rule of thumb, or MRTN) significantly lowers the amount of fertilizer applied per acre with subsequently less NO3 and phosphate losses into drainage systems.
14: <header>Life Cycle Assessment Goal, Scope, and Methods </header>

The goals of this study were (i) to compare fertilization of CC and CS rotations by synthetic fertilizer to the fertilization by swine manure in Iowa using LCA, (ii) to determine the relative availability of manure and crop area in the region, and (iii) to quantify how the uncertainty in manure characteristics and treatment modules affected the results. The ratio of primary nutrients (N,P, and K) in stored manure generally does not match nutrient needs for any given crop or crop rotation. Thus, the functional unit for comparison was not obvious. In this work, we modeled two functional units that apply to each crop system. In the first case, manure was applied in a CC rotation on an N basis at a rate of 17.3 g N kg-1 corn (MRTN). The yield was assumed to be 11.3 Mg ha-1, and the functional unit was fertilization of 1000 kg corn. In the second case, manure was applied on an N basis for a CS rotation, and the functional unit was fertilization of 1000 kg corn and 298 kg soybean. The amount of soybean relative to corn was fixed by the productivity per hectare. When excesses of P and K were applied in the manure, these nutrients were assumed to build up in the soil over time. No credits or emissions were assigned for the excess P and K in this system.
When there was a P deficit in the manure, synthetic fertilizer was added to meet plant needs.

The life cycle boundary is shown in Fig. 1. The two most heavily used housing/storage methods in Iowa (DP and AL) were included as separate cases. The emissions and the nutrient concentration and conservation were quantified in these systems as they apply to swine management in Iowa. Here, we drew the life cycle boundary within the housing system. Energy use for ventilation fans in the building was attributed to hog production and was excluded from the study. Energy use for fans in manure pits was attributed to manure management and was included. During storage of manure and application of synthetic fertilizer and manure to land, there were significant emissions of CH4 N2O, NH, and NO The choices of tillage method (conservation till or no-till), fertilizer type (synthetic or manure), and fertilizer application method (broadcast or injection) affect the direct emission of these chemicals as well as the diesel consumption during tillage and application of nutrients. In this study, we considered only the conservation or no-till method and injection of fertilizer.

Use of manure as a fertilizer is an example of open-loop recycling. The waste from the first product system (animal products) is used in the production of a second product system (crops). In this situation, the reprocessing of waste is often attributed to the second system. Thus, emissions from the housing and storage of manure would be attributed to the crop. In many industrial systems, a recycling credit is given for avoided emissions from an alternate disposal scenario, such as landfill or incineration. These disposal options do not exist for manure because regulations require yearly application to land, and the rate of application must match expected crop utilization of N. In this study, no credit was given for avoided emissions.

There is high uncertainty associated with the characterization of animal waste as excreted and with the chemical transformations and emissions during storage, processing, and application of manure. In the detailed descriptions of these processes that follow, we discuss generic emission values suggested by the Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC) as well as additional emission values from sources with more detailed or location-specific data when available. In each case, the values used in the life cycle study, which are representative for Iowa, are specified. Uncertainty distributions and data ranges were taken from the literature directly or estimated based on available data. Triangular distributions were used for some ranges where upper and lower limits to the value were desired. Lognormal (LGN)
Table 1. Daily manure and nutrient production, as excreted.t
t From Lorimor et al. (2004). # Live weight.
Fig. 1. Activities included in the direct comparison of swine manure processing and application to synthetic fertilizer production and application.

distributions were used in many cases where a value was known to be positive. The LGN distribution is commonly used in LCA for this reason. Normal distributions were used in some cases where the flow could be positive or negative (i.e., for CH4 uptake and release from the soil). The error estimates shown for each emission factor were used in a Monte Carlo uncertainty analysis in Simapro LCA software, version 7.3 (Pré Consultants, 2013). Global parameters were used to track N, P, and K in the manure processing steps. This allowed the amount of manure processed for the CC rotation and the amount of additional synthetic fertilizer in the CS rotation to be directly linked to the uncertainty in manure characteristics and treatment modules. Parameters were also used to incorporate allocation options into the uncertainty analysis for synthetic fertilizer production. Each scenario was calculated with a Monte Carlo simulation with 1000 runs, and the 95% confidence intervals were used as error bars in the results. Results are presented for DP, AL, and synthetic fertilizer for CC and CS rotations. In all cases, no-till/minimal till management was assumed, and the fertilizers were applied by injection. The impact assessment was done with CML 2.05 2001 methodology in SimaPro. The CML method, developed at the Institute of Environmental Sciences (CML) at Leiden University, was chosen because it is a midpoint method with a small but suitable set of categories for this comparison. Additional impact categories were defined for live weight days (kg LW to show the hogs required per unit crop and fossil fuel consumption.

20: The most abundant manure management systems in the Midwest (Iowa, Kansas, Missouri, and Nebraska) are DP (40.2%), AL (38.2%), and liquid slurry (17%) (Thoma et al.,

2011). Primary differences between DPs and ALs are that the DPs are located below hog barns, whereas the (usually uncovered) ALs are generally diluted ponds outside the barns. The liquid slurry systems are undiluted shallow ponds or tanks outside the barns, commonly fed by pull-plug operations. All of these systems have significant NH and CH emissions. Of particular importance for global warming potential are the CH emissions from housing and storage systems. Anaerobic digesters with CH capture for flare or energy recovery are alternative storage methods that can reduce CH) emissions and generate heat and or electricity. The use of a cover can have a significant impact on the LCA results; however, there are currently only 24 swine digesters in the United States and only three anaerobic digesters for all livestock in Iowa (USEPA Agstar, 2010). Thus, these CH, recovery systems were excluded from this study.

Methane emissions are produced when volatile solids in manure are broken down into CH and CO. Typical biogas contains between 55 and 70% CH by volume. The majority of the balance is CO, Biogas formation and resulting emissions were estimated separately for DP and AL systems. For DP systems, the IPCC tier 2 estimates were compared with data from an Iowa farm from Martin (2003). For the AL system, IPCC tier 2 estimates were compared with measurements from several anaerobic lagoons in the United States as reported by Griffing et al. (2004). Table 2 shows emission estimates and ranges for each source. The value and distribution used in the Iowa representative LCA are also specified in Table 2.

The IPCC tier 2 CH4 emissions were estimated based on volatile solids content in manure, temperature, and storage system. The maximum CH4 generation was calculated from the volatile solids (VS) content. For the United States, this value is

estimated to be 0.48 + 0.07 m³ CH4 kg-1 VS (IPCC, 2006). In this study, we used a VS production rate of 5.8 VS LW d-¹ (IPCC, 2006). For comparison, Barker and Overcash (2007) reported a median and standard deviation of 5.6 + 2.4 VS kg - LW d-¹ based on a review of 28 articles, primarily based on studies in the United States.

The percentage of volatile solids that is converted to CH4 emissions depends on the storage method and average temperature (IPCC, 2006). The average yearly temperature in northern Iowa is 7°C, and in southeastern Iowa the average temperature is 11°C (NOAA, 2012). In these calculations, we assumed a temperature of 11°C, and the CH, generation was estimated to be 68% for lagoons and 19% for pit systems with storage for > 1 mo (IPCC, 2006). From these IPCC tier 2 factors, the CH4 emissions for market hogs in Iowa were estimated to be 1.24 and 0.35 g CH4 kg-1 LW d-¹ for AL and DP, respectively.

Griffing et al. (2004) compared CH production from covered and uncovered anaerobic lagoons in Iowa, Oklahoma, and North Carolina. The median and standard deviation were 1.29 and 0.56 g CH kg-1 LW d-1. This value is very close to the IPCC (2006) estimate of 1.24 g CH kg-1 LW d-1. Martin (2003) measured destruction of total volatile solids (TVS) from a deep pit system in a commercial grow to finish swine operation near Ames, Iowa. The TVS destruction was 40%, which is more than twice the IPCC estimate of 19%. The CH, emissions from DPs depend on the length of time and season of storage. The data from Martin (2003) were collected over two hog grow-finish cycles, which is approximately a full year. The IPCC method applies to storage for any length of time > mo. In this study, the goal was to represent conditions in Iowa with yearly application of manure. Thus, we used Martin's measurement of TVS destruction in the IPCC model, which resulted in CH4 emissions of 0.7 g CH4 kg-1 LW d-1. We assumed a triangular error distribution from 0.3 (IPCC estimate) to 1gCH,kg-1 LW d-1.

Ammonia emissions from DP systems were estimated by the IPCC as 25% of excreted N, and the range is specified as 15 to 30. Griffing et al. (2007) reviewed NH3 emissions from 26 experimental studies of slurry pit housing systems. Emissions were 26 and 21% for U.S. and European studies, respectively. However, when studies that were performed during parts of the

year or parts of the day were corrected for seasonal and diurnal variations, emissions were 22 and 21% for U.S. and European studies, respectively. The global mean and 95% confidence interval were 21.2% and 18 to 25%. Ammonia volatility and hence emissions increase with temperature, creating seasonal and diurnal fluctuations. The average summer:winter emissions ratio was 2.2 in the meta-analysis by Griffing et al. (2007). The yearly average temperatures in Iowa are lower than that in most of the study locations included in the average. Thus, an NH3 emission factor on the low side of the recommended range was used. A triangular distribution was used with a peak of 18% and a range of 15 to 25% of excreted N.

The IPCC estimate for volatilization of N from anaerobic lagoons is 40% (range, 25-75%) (IPCC, 2006). Ammonia emissions from anaerobic lagoons have been measured directly by several researchers using dynamic chambers or open-path Fourier transform infrared spectrometers. Griffing et al. (2004) reviewed direct emission measurements and anaerobic lagoon N losses and compared these with a mass balance model fit to data. The mean and 95% confidence interval ranges for NH3-N losses from direct measurements (Aneja et al., 2000, 2001; Todd et al., 2001; Harper et al., 2000; Harper et al., 2004; Lim et al., 2003) and mass balance were 26% (12-41%) and 50% (34-66%), respectively. Ammonia emissions from the lagoon are expected to increase with temperature and wind speed (Harper et al., 2004). Although temperature is lower in Iowa, wind speed is higher. Griffing et al. (2004) did not find significant differences between emissions for the three North Carolina lagoons and the Iowa lagoon. It was not clear whether this inconsistency in N balance between measured NH3 emissions and mass balance measurements was due to measurement errors or to conversion of N to some other species, such as N2. In this LCA, we assumed N emissions from the anaerobic lagoon were 37% (23-51%). Although there is no known mechanism for conversion to N2 in anaerobic conditions, in this LCA we assumed that additional N was emitted as N,, SO that N conservation is consistent with the mass balance model.
Nitrous oxide emissions from the DP and AL were assumed to be 0.002 (0-0.004) and 0.005 (0-0.010) kg N2O-Nk N into the lagoon. These are IPCC tier 2 estimates (IPCC, 2006).

injection tanker tractor traveled up to 3.2 km to the application field, injected the slurry, and returned to the farm in about 20 min. The pumping tractor was a Case IH 5088 with a 100 kW (1361 power take-off, which was estimated to consume 341 (8.9 gallons) diesel h-1. The net pumping time for the year was 45 h, and 7.57 million L of manure was pumped. On a unit volume manure basis, this was 1.83e-4 L diesel L-1 manure. The diesel consumption per ha for the DP case .9 gNL-1) was 9 L ha-1.

The IPCC tier 2 CH4 emissions were estimated based on volatile solids content in manure, temperature, and storage system. The maximum CH4 generation was calculated from the volatile solids (VS) content. For the United States, this value is

estimated to be 0.48 + 0.07 m³ CH4 kg-1 VS (IPCC, 2006). In this study, we used a VS production rate of 5.8 VS LW d-¹ (IPCC, 2006). For comparison, Barker and Overcash (2007) reported a median and standard deviation of 5.6 + 2.4 VS kg - LW d-¹ based on a review of 28 articles, primarily based on studies in the United States.

The percentage of volatile solids that is converted to CH4 emissions depends on the storage method and average temperature (IPCC, 2006). The average yearly temperature in northern Iowa is 7°C, and in southeastern Iowa the average temperature is 11°C (NOAA, 2012). In these calculations, we assumed a temperature of 11°C, and the CH, generation was estimated to be 68% for lagoons and 19% for pit systems with storage for > 1 mo (IPCC, 2006). From these IPCC tier 2 factors, the CH4 emissions for market hogs in Iowa were estimated to be 1.24 and 0.35 g CH4 kg-1 LW d-¹ for AL and DP, respectively.

Griffing et al. (2004) compared CH production from covered and uncovered anaerobic lagoons in Iowa, Oklahoma, and North Carolina. The median and standard deviation were 1.29 and 0.56 g CH kg-1 LW d-1. This value is very close to the IPCC (2006) estimate of 1.24 g CH kg-1 LW d-1. Martin (2003) measured destruction of total volatile solids (TVS) from a deep pit system in a commercial grow to finish swine operation near Ames, Iowa. The TVS destruction was 40%, which is more than twice the IPCC estimate of 19%. The CH, emissions from DPs depend on the length of time and season of storage. The data from Martin (2003) were collected over two hog grow-finish cycles, which is approximately a full year. The IPCC method applies to storage for any length of time > mo. In this study, the goal was to represent conditions in Iowa with yearly application of manure. Thus, we used Martin's measurement of TVS destruction in the IPCC model, which resulted in CH4 emissions of 0.7 g CH4 kg-1 LW d-1. We assumed a triangular error distribution from 0.3 (IPCC estimate) to 1gCH,kg-1 LW d-1.

Ammonia emissions from DP systems were estimated by the IPCC as 25% of excreted N, and the range is specified as 15 to 30. Griffing et al. (2007) reviewed NH3 emissions from 26 experimental studies of slurry pit housing systems. Emissions were 26 and 21% for U.S. and European studies, respectively. However, when studies that were performed during parts of the

year or parts of the day were corrected for seasonal and diurnal variations, emissions were 22 and 21% for U.S. and European studies, respectively. The global mean and 95% confidence interval were 21.2% and 18 to 25%. Ammonia volatility and hence emissions increase with temperature, creating seasonal and diurnal fluctuations. The average summer:winter emissions ratio was 2.2 in the meta-analysis by Griffing et al. (2007). The yearly average temperatures in Iowa are lower than that in most of the study locations included in the average. Thus, an NH3 emission factor on the low side of the recommended range was used. A triangular distribution was used with a peak of 18% and a range of 15 to 25% of excreted N.

The IPCC estimate for volatilization of N from anaerobic lagoons is 40% (range, 25-75%) (IPCC, 2006). Ammonia emissions from anaerobic lagoons have been measured directly by several researchers using dynamic chambers or open-path Fourier transform infrared spectrometers. Griffing et al. (2004) reviewed direct emission measurements and anaerobic lagoon N losses and compared these with a mass balance model fit to data. The mean and 95% confidence interval ranges for NH3-N losses from direct measurements (Aneja et al., 2000, 2001; Todd et al., 2001; Harper et al., 2000; Harper et al., 2004; Lim et al., 2003) and mass balance were 26% (12-41%) and 50% (34-66%), respectively. Ammonia emissions from the lagoon are expected to increase with temperature and wind speed (Harper et al., 2004). Although temperature is lower in Iowa, wind speed is higher. Griffing et al. (2004) did not find significant differences between emissions for the three North Carolina lagoons and the Iowa lagoon. It was not clear whether this inconsistency in N balance between measured NH3 emissions and mass balance measurements was due to measurement errors or to conversion of N to some other species, such as N2. In this LCA, we assumed N emissions from the anaerobic lagoon were 37% (23-51%). Although there is no known mechanism for conversion to N2 in anaerobic conditions, in this LCA we assumed that additional N was emitted as N,, SO that N conservation is consistent with the mass balance model.
Nitrous oxide emissions from the DP and AL were assumed to be 0.002 (0-0.004) and 0.005 (0-0.010) kg N2O-Nk N into the lagoon. These are IPCC tier 2 estimates (IPCC, 2006).

injection tanker tractor traveled up to 3.2 km to the application field, injected the slurry, and returned to the farm in about 20 min. The pumping tractor was a Case IH 5088 with a 100 kW (1361 power take-off, which was estimated to consume 341 (8.9 gallons) diesel h-1. The net pumping time for the year was 45 h, and 7.57 million L of manure was pumped. On a unit volume manure basis, this was 1.83e-4 L diesel L-1 manure. The diesel consumption per ha for the DP case .9 gNL-1) was 9 L ha-1.
25: The most abundant manure management systems in the Midwest (Iowa, Kansas, Missouri, and Nebraska) are DP (40.2%), AL (38.2%), and liquid slurry (17%) (Thoma et al.,

2011). Primary differences between DPs and ALs are that the DPs are located below hog barns, whereas the (usually uncovered) ALs are generally diluted ponds outside the barns. The liquid slurry systems are undiluted shallow ponds or tanks outside the barns, commonly fed by pull-plug operations. All of these systems have significant NH and CH emissions. Of particular importance for global warming potential are the CH emissions from housing and storage systems. Anaerobic digesters with CH capture for flare or energy recovery are alternative storage methods that can reduce CH) emissions and generate heat and or electricity. The use of a cover can have a significant impact on the LCA results; however, there are currently only 24 swine digesters in the United States and only three anaerobic digesters for all livestock in Iowa (USEPA Agstar, 2010). Thus, these CH, recovery systems were excluded from this study.

From these IPCC tier 2 factors, the CH4 emissions for market hogs in Iowa were estimated to be 1.24 and 0.35 g CH4 kg-1 LW d-¹ for AL and DP, respectively.

Griffing et al. (2004) compared CH production from covered and uncovered anaerobic lagoons in Iowa, Oklahoma, and North Carolina. The median and standard deviation were 1.29 and 0.56 g CH kg-1 LW d-1. This value is very close to the IPCC (2006) estimate of 1.24 g CH kg-1 LW d-1. Martin (2003) measured destruction of total volatile solids (TVS) from a deep pit system in a commercial grow to finish swine operation near Ames, Iowa. The TVS destruction was 40%, which is more than twice the IPCC estimate of 19%. The CH, emissions from DPs depend on the length of time and season of storage. The data from Martin (2003) were collected over two hog grow-finish cycles, which is approximately a full year. The IPCC method applies to storage for any length of time > mo. In this study, the goal was to represent conditions in Iowa with yearly application of manure. Thus, we used Martin's measurement of TVS destruction in the IPCC model, which resulted in CH4 emissions of 0.7 g CH4 kg-1 LW d-1. We assumed a triangular error distribution from 0.3 (IPCC estimate) to 1gCH,kg-1 LW d-1.

Ammonia emissions from DP systems were estimated by the IPCC as 25% of excreted N, and the range is specified as 15 to 30. Griffing et al. (2007) reviewed NH3 emissions from 26 experimental studies of slurry pit housing systems. Emissions were 26 and 21% for U.S. and European studies, respectively. However, when studies that were performed during parts of the

year or parts of the day were corrected for seasonal and diurnal variations, emissions were 22 and 21% for U.S. and European studies, respectively. The global mean and 95% confidence interval were 21.2% and 18 to 25%. Ammonia volatility and hence emissions increase with temperature, creating seasonal and diurnal fluctuations. The average summer:winter emissions ratio was 2.2 in the meta-analysis by Griffing et al. (2007). The yearly average temperatures in Iowa are lower than that in most of the study locations included in the average. Thus, an NH3 emission factor on the low side of the recommended range was used. A triangular distribution was used with a peak of 18% and a range of 15 to 25% of excreted N.

The IPCC estimate for volatilization of N from anaerobic lagoons is 40% (range, 25-75%) (IPCC, 2006). Ammonia emissions from anaerobic lagoons have been measured directly by several researchers using dynamic chambers or open-path Fourier transform infrared spectrometers. Griffing et al. (2004) reviewed direct emission measurements and anaerobic lagoon N losses and compared these with a mass balance model fit to data. The mean and 95% confidence interval ranges for NH3-N losses from direct measurements (Aneja et al., 2000, 2001; Todd et al., 2001; Harper et al., 2000; Harper et al., 2004; Lim et al., 2003) and mass balance were 26% (12-41%) and 50% (34-66%), respectively. Ammonia emissions from the lagoon are expected to increase with temperature and wind speed (Harper et al., 2004). Although temperature is lower in Iowa, wind speed is higher. Griffing et al. (2004) did not find significant differences between emissions for the three North Carolina lagoons and the Iowa lagoon. It was not clear whether this inconsistency in N balance between measured NH3 emissions and mass balance measurements was due to measurement errors or to conversion of N to some other species, such as N2. In this LCA, we assumed N emissions from the anaerobic lagoon were 37% (23-51%). Although there is no known mechanism for conversion to N2 in anaerobic conditions, in this LCA we assumed that additional N was emitted as N,, SO that N conservation is consistent with the mass balance model.
Nitrous oxide emissions from the DP and AL were assumed to be 0.002 (0-0.004) and 0.005 (0-0.010) kg N2O-Nk N into the lagoon. These are IPCC tier 2 estimates (IPCC, 2006).

injection tanker tractor traveled up to 3.2 km to the application field, injected the slurry, and returned to the farm in about 20 min. The pumping tractor was a Case IH 5088 with a 100 kW (1361 power take-off, which was estimated to consume 341 (8.9 gallons) diesel h-1. The net pumping time for the year was 45 h, and 7.57 million L of manure was pumped. On a unit volume manure basis, this was 1.83e-4 L diesel L-1 manure. The diesel consumption per ha for the DP case .9 gNL-1) was 9 L ha-1.
29: <header>Energy Use in Storage, Handling, Transport, and Application </header>

In the Story County case study farm, aboveground fans are used to provide ventilation in the house for the hogs. These fans are attributed to growing the hogs and not waste management. Additional fans are used to evacuate air from the pits. These fans run continuously throughout the year, including the period between growth cycles. In the case study farm, there were 24 fans, each with separate motors rated at 2 amps on single-phase 230 V power. Thus, the rating was 3.48e8 kJ yr-1. The motors were assumed to draw 70% (normal distribution SD2 = 30) of the rated power. The case study farm had two barns with a capacity of 2300 hogs. We assumed that these barns were at 100% capacity for 345 d yr-1, and the average hog weight during the growth cycle was 70 kg. Thus, the electricity use was 2.2 kJ kg-1 LW d-¹ in the Iowa representative model. Electricity use in the barn was modeled with an Iowa-specific electricity grid from the Iowa Utility Board (IUB, 2013).

Estimates for diesel consumption for pumping and land application of manure were 9 and 23 L ha-1 respectively. These estimates were measurements from the case study farm, where manure was applied within 3.2 km of the storage facility. Diesel consumption for application of synthetic fertilizer was assumed to be 2.7 L ha- The Iowa extension (IA Extension, 2001) stated fuel consumption as 1.41 ha-¹ for dry fertilizer and 5.1 ha-1 for anhydrous NH3 application.

31: The most common land application methods are surface spreading and subsurface injection (Dell et al., 2011). Subsurface injection is preferred or required in many states; therefore, we used subsurface injection in this study.

In the case study farm, manure was removed from storage facilities and applied to land once per year. Long-distance transport of manure is expensive, SO manure is typically applied on nearby fields. On the basis of personal communications, 24 km was chosen as a representative maximum distance that manure would need to be transported. This is consistent with intracounty transport, with individual counties in Iowa being 1460 km2 or roughly 40 km wide. Kellogg et al. (2000) showed that most counties had an excess of N and P in manure at many individual farms. However, at the county level, the manure had <25% of the N and P uptake by plants in all counties. Thus, off-farm transport is common. However, transport over county-wide distances is not needed. Updated data from the USDA National Agricultural Statistical Service (NASS, 2012) shows that as of 2008, the three counties with the highest hog population per ha of planted crops (Hardin, Washington, and Sioux) had about 0.14 ha hog Thus, in these counties, there is more than enough cropland to accept the manure for a CC rotation. However, if swine waste were applied directly (no N losses in storage) for a CS rotation, the area required would be greater than the available land. Story County had 1.7 ha hog-1 about 10 times the required area.

Emissions of vary by year and soil conditions. Chantigny et al. (2010) reported emissions from injected swine manure ranging from 3.1 to 4.8% of applied N for a clay soil. This is similar to Smith et al. (2011). Jarecki et al. (2008) reported values in a laboratory-controlled study of 1.84% for a clay soil in Iowa. Emissions from injected urea ammonium nitrate (UAN) ranged from 0.24 to 6.6% of applied N (Jarecki et al., 2008; Chantigny et al., 2010). Eichner (1990) reported N2O emissions ranging from 0.01 to 6.84% of applied fertilizer N, with the highest percentages observed in Iowa soils. Parkin and Kaspar (2006) reported 3.9% for corn with surface-applied UAN. Venturea et al. (2005) reported 2.4% for injected N in no-till. In this LCA study, N2O emissions for application were set at 3.5% for swine manure and 2.7% for synthetic fertilizer. Emissions and error distributions for emissions from the soil are in Table 3.

34: Common sources of synthetic N are NH urea, ammonium nitrate, or combinations of these. Common sources of P are diammonium phosphate and triple superphosphate (TSP). Potassium (K) is typically supplied as potassium chloride (KCI). In this study, we discuss production of each of these synthetic fertilizers. Urea ammonium nitrate is commonly and increasingly used as an N fertilizer in this region. Urea ammonium nitrate, TSP, and KCl were used in the comparison to swine manure.

In the LCA model, we considered two cases for transport. In the first case, the manure was applied at or near the hog farm. Thus, the material was pumped directly into the land application equipment. In the second case, all of the stored manure was pumped into a long-distance transport vessel, transported 24 km, and then pumped into the land application equipment. Transportation emissions are often specified on a ton-km basis (tkm). In the LCA model, the N concentrations after storage were 3.9 g N L-1 (DP) and 1.5 g (AL). The error estimates were LGN 1.5 and 3, respectively. Fuel consumption for truck tractors in the United States is about 1 MJ tkm-1 transported (USLCI, 2012; Overcash and Griffing, 2012). Using the application rate for CC (195 kgN ha-1), these estimates of concentration and truck transport energy resulted in fuel consumptions for long-distance transport of 32 and 82 L ha for DP and AL storage, respectively.

Environmental Clarity (Overcash and Griffing, 2012) maintains a life cycle database of about 1000 chemicals. These data were developed using a design-based method described by Jimenez-Gonzalez and Overcash (2000). Each gate-to-gate (GTG) manufacturing process is modeled based on standard engineering calculations. These data were used to model synthetic fertilizer production in this LCA. Electricity consumption for fertilizer production was from the U.S. average grid. To model uncertainty in the synthetic fertilizer supply chain, we assumed that each flow had an LGN distribution. In each gate-to-gate, material inputs are specified by stoichiometry and yield information, which are relatively well known in the chemical industry. For these inputs, the

Pumping diesel was calculated using on data from the case study farm. The power take-off of a pumping tractor was used to power a sump pump, which loaded manure slurry into a tanker tractor. This operation took 10 min to fill a 28.4-m3 injection tanker. The

SD2 was set to 1.05. Carbon dioxide emissions of significant size, such as those in the NH2 process, are typically known as accurately as the material inputs. Thus, an LGN distribution with an error of SD2 = 1.05 was used. Other emissions are typically caused by side reactions, byproduct reactions, and fugitive losses. The side reactions and extent of yield loss are often known and reported. However, the liquid and solid waste streams are typically treated, and the resulting emissions are not well defined. The magnitude of fugitive emissions is also not well known. For these emissions, a log normal distribution was used with SD2 = 2. Energy inputs are typically known to within +30%, and we used an LGN distribution with SD2 = 1.3 for each energy input. Because energy recovery was tabulated separately, the net energy can switch signs during simulations for processes with small net energy.

Nitrogen fertilizers are comprised of NH, which is typically manufactured by the Haber-Bosch process. Although this process is well described in the literature (Eggeman, 2010; Topham, 2000), the allocation of environmental flows to the NH3 and the CO by-product is not often discussed in articles on crop production. The approximately 1.2 kg CO, co-produced per kilogram NH3 has been treated as both a co-product and an emission by various life cycle databases (Nielsen et al., 2003; Frischknecht et al., 2007). In some cases, the allocation to CO, has not been well documented. Kim and Dale (2004, 2005) showed that the impact of CO 2 allocation on N-fertilizer emissions is large. About half of the CO 2 from the reforming process is recovered and used industrially (Topham, 2000). Of this, half is used at the point of production to produce urea or methanol or in enhanced oil recovery processes (CO2 injection into oil wells). The remainder of recovered CO2 is liquefied or solidified to enable transport. Other commercial uses include an inert atmosphere for food transportation, carbonation, freezing, welding, heat transfer in nuclear plants, and aerosols. Ammonia and hydrogen production are the primary sources of CO2 production. Thus, it is important to consider the allocation of this co-product. In this study, we set up an allocation parameter, which ranged from 0 to 1. When zero, the allocation was done by mass, and the process CO2 emissions were set to zero. When set to 1, 100% of the burdens were allocated to NH, and all of the CO2 2 produced was counted as an emission to the environment. In the Monte Carlo simulations, this parameter was set to 0.5 with a uniform distribution between zero and one.
Urea ammonium nitrate is a liquid comprised of urea and ammonium nitrate. In this study, we assumed a typical mixture

of 238 kg urea and 426 kg ammonium nitrate per Mg UAN. Ammonium nitrate is produced by oxidizing NH, to nitric acid and then neutralizing the nitric acid with NH3. The nitric acid process generates enough N2O as an oxidation byproduct to significantly affect the GWP of UAN. The N2O emissions in uncontrolled plants are about 0.009 kg N,O kg- nitric acid (IPCC, 2006), which is about 11 kg CO2e N in nitric acid. According to the USEPA (2010), these emissions can be reduced 70 to 90% through emission controls. In the Environmental Clarity model used in this study, emissions are 0.0019 kg N20kg-1 of pure nitric acid (80% reduction by controls). This is equivalent to 2.5 kg CO2eq kg- N in nitric acid or 0.45 kg CO2eqkg N in UAN.

Urea is produced by reactingNH with CO. 2 This temporarily stores the CO2 2 until it is released after application to the field. From a life cycle accounting perspective, the CO 2 emissions should be counted as part of the urea cradle to gate inventory or during the land application step. In this study, the default process emissions were reduced to 50% of the total process CO, produced in part due to the CO, 2 used to produce urea. Thus, the CO, sequestration has been included, and CO, emissions on urea application were also included.

Phosphate fertilizers are derived from phosphate rock, which is mined in large quantity in Florida and North Carolina. World production is dominated by the United States, China, and Morocco/Western Sahara (Keyzer, 2010). Reserves are dominated by these regions and by Algeria, Syria, South Africa, and Jordan (Neset Cordell, 2012). Phosphorus is an abundant element, but quantities of highly concentrated phosphate rock that are economically mined and converted to fertilizer are finite. Peak P, defined as a point at which P production rates will peak, is predicted to occur some point between 2030 and 2060 (Neset and Cordell, 2012). MacDonald et al. (2011) estimated that world agronomic inputs of P fertilizer are 14.2 Tg P yr-1 from fertilizer and 9.6 Tg P yr from manure. Phosphorus removal by crops was estimated to be 12.3 Tg P yr-1, or about 50% of the total P applied. Thus, it is important to consider efficient P utilization in fertilization plans.

Phosphate fertilizers use sulfuric acid to digest phosphate rock and to produce phosphoric acid. The sulfuric acid is produced by burning sulfur (S), which is currently produced as a byproduct of oil refining. Thus, some allocation must be made to calculate the life cycle inventory of S. Due to lower S limits in fuel (to reduce SO emissions), S is produced in excess of demand. Thus,

in some databases, S has no environmental burdens. From a CO2 emissions perspective, the allocation has little impact. However, on an abiotic depletion or fossil fuel consumption basis, the allocation of S dominates. For example, per kg the fossil energy input is 7 MJ kg-1 excluding S production. Using mass allocation, the oil required to produce the sulfur used to produce this TSP has a higher heating value of 41 MJ kg-1 P2O5, which is comparable to the energy per kg of N. In this study, the oil refinery is allocated by mass; thus, each kilogram of S consumed requires production of 1 kg of refinery product.

The cradle-to-gate global warming potential from several data sources and allocation methods are compared for four synthetic fertilizers in Table 4. The allocation method in the NH3/CO2 process and treatment of CO sequestration by the urea process has a large impact on the result for NH3 and UAN.

The sulfuric acid is produced by burning sulfur (S), which is currently produced as a byproduct of oil refining. Thus, some allocation must be made to calculate the life cycle inventory of S. Due to lower S limits in fuel (to reduce SO emissions), S is produced in excess of demand. Thus,

in some databases, S has no environmental burdens. From a CO2 emissions perspective, the allocation has little impact. However, on an abiotic depletion or fossil fuel consumption basis, the allocation of S dominates. For example, per kg the fossil energy input is 7 MJ kg-1 excluding S production. Using mass allocation, the oil required to produce the sulfur used to produce this TSP has a higher heating value of 41 MJ kg-1 P2O5, which is comparable to the energy per kg of N. In this study, the oil refinery is allocated by mass; thus, each kilogram of S consumed requires production of 1 kg of refinery product.

The cradle-to-gate global warming potential from several data sources and allocation methods are compared for four synthetic fertilizers in Table 4. The allocation method in the NH3/CO2 process and treatment of CO sequestration by the urea process has a large impact on the result for NH3 and UAN.
36: Common sources of synthetic N are NH urea, ammonium nitrate, or combinations of these. Common sources of P are diammonium phosphate and triple superphosphate (TSP). Potassium (K) is typically supplied as potassium chloride (KCI). In this study, we discuss production of each of these synthetic fertilizers. Urea ammonium nitrate is commonly and increasingly used as an N fertilizer in this region. Urea ammonium nitrate, TSP, and KCl were used in the comparison to swine manure.

In the LCA model, we considered two cases for transport. In the first case, the manure was applied at or near the hog farm. Thus, the material was pumped directly into the land application equipment. In the second case, all of the stored manure was pumped into a long-distance transport vessel, transported 24 km, and then pumped into the land application equipment. Transportation emissions are often specified on a ton-km basis (tkm). In the LCA model, the N concentrations after storage were 3.9 g N L-1 (DP) and 1.5 g (AL). The error estimates were LGN 1.5 and 3, respectively. Fuel consumption for truck tractors in the United States is about 1 MJ tkm-1 transported (USLCI, 2012; Overcash and Griffing, 2012). Using the application rate for CC (195 kgN ha-1), these estimates of concentration and truck transport energy resulted in fuel consumptions for long-distance transport of 32 and 82 L ha for DP and AL storage, respectively.

Environmental Clarity (Overcash and Griffing, 2012) maintains a life cycle database of about 1000 chemicals. These data were developed using a design-based method described by Jimenez-Gonzalez and Overcash (2000). Each gate-to-gate (GTG) manufacturing process is modeled based on standard engineering calculations. These data were used to model synthetic fertilizer production in this LCA. Electricity consumption for fertilizer production was from the U.S. average grid. To model uncertainty in the synthetic fertilizer supply chain, we assumed that each flow had an LGN distribution. In each gate-to-gate, material inputs are specified by stoichiometry and yield information, which are relatively well known in the chemical industry. For these inputs, the

Pumping diesel was calculated using on data from the case study farm. The power take-off of a pumping tractor was used to power a sump pump, which loaded manure slurry into a tanker tractor. This operation took 10 min to fill a 28.4-m3 injection tanker. The

SD2 was set to 1.05. Carbon dioxide emissions of significant size, such as those in the NH2 process, are typically known as accurately as the material inputs. Thus, an LGN distribution with an error of SD2 = 1.05 was used. Other emissions are typically caused by side reactions, byproduct reactions, and fugitive losses. The side reactions and extent of yield loss are often known and reported. However, the liquid and solid waste streams are typically treated, and the resulting emissions are not well defined. The magnitude of fugitive emissions is also not well known. For these emissions, a log normal distribution was used with SD2 = 2. Energy inputs are typically known to within +30%, and we used an LGN distribution with SD2 = 1.3 for each energy input. Because energy recovery was tabulated separately, the net energy can switch signs during simulations for processes with small net energy.

Nitrogen fertilizers are comprised of NH, which is typically manufactured by the Haber-Bosch process. Although this process is well described in the literature (Eggeman, 2010; Topham, 2000), the allocation of environmental flows to the NH3 and the CO by-product is not often discussed in articles on crop production. The approximately 1.2 kg CO, co-produced per kilogram NH3 has been treated as both a co-product and an emission by various life cycle databases (Nielsen et al., 2003; Frischknecht et al., 2007). In some cases, the allocation to CO, has not been well documented. Kim and Dale (2004, 2005) showed that the impact of CO 2 allocation on N-fertilizer emissions is large. About half of the CO 2 from the reforming process is recovered and used industrially (Topham, 2000). Of this, half is used at the point of production to produce urea or methanol or in enhanced oil recovery processes (CO2 injection into oil wells). The remainder of recovered CO2 is liquefied or solidified to enable transport. Other commercial uses include an inert atmosphere for food transportation, carbonation, freezing, welding, heat transfer in nuclear plants, and aerosols. Ammonia and hydrogen production are the primary sources of CO2 production. Thus, it is important to consider the allocation of this co-product. In this study, we set up an allocation parameter, which ranged from 0 to 1. When zero, the allocation was done by mass, and the process CO2 emissions were set to zero. When set to 1, 100% of the burdens were allocated to NH, and all of the CO2 2 produced was counted as an emission to the environment. In the Monte Carlo simulations, this parameter was set to 0.5 with a uniform distribution between zero and one.
Urea ammonium nitrate is a liquid comprised of urea and ammonium nitrate. In this study, we assumed a typical mixture

of 238 kg urea and 426 kg ammonium nitrate per Mg UAN. Ammonium nitrate is produced by oxidizing NH, to nitric acid and then neutralizing the nitric acid with NH3. The nitric acid process generates enough N2O as an oxidation byproduct to significantly affect the GWP of UAN. The N2O emissions in uncontrolled plants are about 0.009 kg N,O kg- nitric acid (IPCC, 2006), which is about 11 kg CO2e N in nitric acid. According to the USEPA (2010), these emissions can be reduced 70 to 90% through emission controls. In the Environmental Clarity model used in this study, emissions are 0.0019 kg N20kg-1 of pure nitric acid (80% reduction by controls). This is equivalent to 2.5 kg CO2eq kg- N in nitric acid or 0.45 kg CO2eqkg N in UAN.

Urea is produced by reactingNH with CO. 2 This temporarily stores the CO2 2 until it is released after application to the field. From a life cycle accounting perspective, the CO 2 emissions should be counted as part of the urea cradle to gate inventory or during the land application step. In this study, the default process emissions were reduced to 50% of the total process CO, produced in part due to the CO, 2 used to produce urea. Thus, the CO, sequestration has been included, and CO, emissions on urea application were also included.

Phosphate fertilizers are derived from phosphate rock, which is mined in large quantity in Florida and North Carolina. World production is dominated by the United States, China, and Morocco/Western Sahara (Keyzer, 2010). Reserves are dominated by these regions and by Algeria, Syria, South Africa, and Jordan (Neset Cordell, 2012). Phosphorus is an abundant element, but quantities of highly concentrated phosphate rock that are economically mined and converted to fertilizer are finite. Peak P, defined as a point at which P production rates will peak, is predicted to occur some point between 2030 and 2060 (Neset and Cordell, 2012). MacDonald et al. (2011) estimated that world agronomic inputs of P fertilizer are 14.2 Tg P yr-1 from fertilizer and 9.6 Tg P yr from manure. Phosphorus removal by crops was estimated to be 12.3 Tg P yr-1, or about 50% of the total P applied. Thus, it is important to consider efficient P utilization in fertilization plans.
