is a byproduct of oil production. Although P consumption has been identified as an important nonrenewable resource, it does not show up in the CML method.

The GWP is an important impact category in this study, and there were several decisions in the methodology and life cycle inventory that affected the overall comparison. The Iowa representative CH emission factor from the DP was assumed to be larger than the IPCC tier 2 value. The N2O emissions from the field were assumed to be higher for manure than for UAN application. However, these emissions are considered to be equal in the IPCC methodology. In addition, some of the emissions in the NH3 production process were allocated to CO2. In the comparison, the allocation factor was allowed to vary, SO that the impact of this choice was included in the error bars. All of these choices increased the GWP of manure relative to synthetic fertilizer. The impact of these methodological and impact factor choices on GWP is shown in Fig. 8. For UAN, the allocation was set all to NH3 or allowed to vary between all to NH3 and mass allocation. For the DP case, the CH, emissions were set to the Iowa representative value of 0.7 g kg-1 LW d-¹ or the IPCC value of 0.35 g kg- LW d-¹. For each of these cases, the N2O emissions from the field were set to be equal as described by the IPCC or set to the Iowa representative values (Jarecki et al., 2008; Chantigny et al., 2010). Setting the CH4 emission factor equal to the IPCC value resulted in a large reduction in GWP for the DP case. Changing the allocation method in synthetic fertilizer production resulted in a significant change when using the IPCC N20 emission factor (1.0%). However, the relative change is less with the Iowa representative N,O emissions (Jarecki et al., 2008; Chantigny et al., 2010). This change was also small relative to the difference between the DP and synthetic emission factors. Changing the N2O emission factors from the Iowa representative and IPCC values made a significant difference. However, the comparison between the DP and synthetic fertilizer is not materially changed by this choice. Under all of the scenarios considered, synthetic fertilizer showed significantly lower emissions than the stored manure.

There have been some efforts to achieve this (USEPA Agstar, 2010), but these are not common practice. Nitrous oxide emissions from the field may be mitigated by adding biochar to storage units or applying it with fertilizers (Steiner et al., 2010; Taghizadeh-Toosi, 2011; Taghizadeh-Toosi et al., 2012). A better understanding of the impact of soil conditions, application timing, and rate may yield N2 emission reductions as well.

Matching nutrients in manure with crop needs is important for efficient utilization of nutrients and for prevention of P build-up in soil. The best crop rotation for nutrient matching depends on residual P levels in the soil, manure analysis, and storage method. Manure in concert with synthetic fertilizers and crop rotation is effective, economical, and low risk if storage and application follow best management practices and soil nutrient assays coupled with manure nutrient composition are used to determine rates of application. This method is common practice in Iowa and was used on the case study farm.

The hog population of Iowa is approximately 20 million market hogs and breeders. Using best management practices and amending with synthetic fertilizers for a CS rotation, these hogs produce enough manure to fertilize 3.24 million ha (DP, MRTN) or 1.62 million ha (DP, P index) of Iowa's total farm acreage, which is approximately 12.5 million ha. Thus, on a state-wide basis, hog manure can be efficiently used with a small fraction of the total available land. A similar analysis including other types of livestock and crops would provide a more complete picture of the nutrient balance in Iowa.

However, from a global warming perspective, the impact of transport was small relative to the impact of manure CH and N2O emissions from storage and the field. In long-distance transport situations, the optimum yield economics are altered, and there may be an incentive to overapply manure. Overapplication could also lead to increased eutrophication, acidification, and GWP. Thus, the economic and ecologic optimum application rates may diverge for regions of high hog population.

For synthetic fertilizer (4b), the largest contributions to global warming and eutrophication impact were during land application (N2O emissions and NO leaching). In other categories, particularly acidification, the majority of emissions are from N fertilizer production (UAN). Triple superphosphate production contributes about 10 to 20% in many impact categories. Seventy-five percent of the fossil fuel consumption and abiotic depletion associated with TSP production was from sulfur, which is a byproduct of oil refining.

The synthetic fertilizer was compared with manure (DP and AL storage) for a CC rotation (Fig. 5). Error bars in Fig. 5 represent the 95% interval from Monte Carlo simulations. Global warming impacts for synthetic fertilization were the

Fig. Global warming potential (GWP) by chemical emission and production step for deep pit storage with 24-km long distance transport (LDT) to field. Field, field emissions; HD, housing direct emissions; HE, housing energy emissions.
lowest. When storage systems were compared, the DP system had 50% less GWP than the AL system. This comparison was due to reduction in CH emissions from the storage system and conservation of N.

The acidification and eutrophication impacts, which were strongly influenced by NH3 emissions from housing, are significantly lower for DP than for AL. The DP was assumed to have NH, emissions of 18% (15-25%), versus 37% (23-51%) for the AL. These improvements were amplified by the conservation of N. On a unit crop production basis, more live weight was required for the AL than for the DP, as shown by the live weight category. Consequently, the amount of N treated and associated acidification, eutrophication, GWP, and photochemical oxidation impacts were higher. The live weight needed was 57 + 15 kg d LW kg- corn for AL and 36 + 8 kg d LW kg-1 corn for DP. In Fig. 5, the error bars for live weight reflect relatively large uncertainties in the swine waste characterization data (N production per unit live weight) as well as the emissions of N in storage. However, variations in swine waste characteristics do not affect the comparison between storage methods. Thus, the error bars here are indicative of the certainty between manure and synthetic fertilizer and not between storage methods.

On a unit product basis, however, the N applied in the CS rotation is much lower and contributes to lower GWP emissions. Important LCA impact categories in manure and synthetic fertilization are fossil energy consumption, GWP, eutrophication, and acidification (Reckmann et al., 2012). Figure 7 combines information from Fig. 4 and 5 for important categories of the life cycle analysis for the continuous corn rotation. For each nutrient source, Fig. 7 shows the relative size of the emissions by stage, which can be used to estimate the contribution of each emission to the overall uncertainty in Fig. 5 and 6.

Eutrophication from synthetic fertilization was dominated by NO leaching from the field (80%) and cradle-to-gate production (20%). Eutrophication from the manure fertilization was dominated by NH emissions from housing/storage (60%) and field emissions (40%). Leaching of N was estimated to be 24% of total synthetic N (Lawlor et al., 2008) applied and 9.6% of total organic N applied (Gupta et al., 2004). Nitrogen in manure is considered to be released slower than that UAN, and this can lead to lower expected leaching. The higher leaching rate for synthetic fertilizers from the field roughly made up for the eutrophication impact of NH emissions from housing/storage. Given the large variability in these data, eutrophication potential is equally affected by manures and synthetic fertilizers.

Acidification was dominated by NH emissions from housing/storage. These emissions represented 97% of the total acidification for the DP. The acidification emissions for synthetic fertilizer were about 11% of those for the DP case. These were dominated by energy-related emissions in the production of UAN.

The life cycle impacts of abiotic depletion (depletion of nonliving natural resources) and nonrenewable fossil energy use are nearly identical in this analysis. For synthetic fertilizer, these are dominated by cradle-to-gate production. Nitrogen fertilizer uses the largest amount of fuel, and P-based fertilizers contribute about 20%. The N fertilizer fossil inputs were due to natural gas use, and the P fuel use was due to consumption of sulfur, which

is a byproduct of oil production. Although P consumption has been identified as an important nonrenewable resource, it does not show up in the CML method.

The GWP is an important impact category in this study, and there were several decisions in the methodology and life cycle inventory that affected the overall comparison. The Iowa representative CH emission factor from the DP was assumed to be larger than the IPCC tier 2 value. The N2O emissions from the field were assumed to be higher for manure than for UAN application. However, these emissions are considered to be equal in the IPCC methodology. In addition, some of the emissions in the NH3 production process were allocated to CO2. In the comparison, the allocation factor was allowed to vary, SO that the impact of this choice was included in the error bars. All of these choices increased the GWP of manure relative to synthetic fertilizer. The impact of these methodological and impact factor choices on GWP is shown in Fig. 8. For UAN, the allocation was set all to NH3 or allowed to vary between all to NH3 and mass allocation. For the DP case, the CH, emissions were set to the Iowa representative value of 0.7 g kg-1 LW d-¹ or the IPCC value of 0.35 g kg- LW d-¹. For each of these cases, the N2O emissions from the field were set to be equal as described by the IPCC or set to the Iowa representative values (Jarecki et al., 2008; Chantigny et al., 2010). Setting the CH4 emission factor equal to the IPCC value resulted in a large reduction in GWP for the DP case. Changing the allocation method in synthetic fertilizer production resulted in a significant change when using the IPCC N20 emission factor (1.0%). However, the relative change is less with the Iowa representative N,O emissions (Jarecki et al., 2008; Chantigny et al., 2010). This change was also small relative to the difference between the DP and synthetic emission factors. Changing the N2O emission factors from the Iowa representative and IPCC values made a significant difference. However, the comparison between the DP and synthetic fertilizer is not materially changed by this choice. Under all of the scenarios considered, synthetic fertilizer showed significantly lower emissions than the stored manure.

In order for manure to be competitive with synthetic fertilizer with respect to GWP, a reduction in CH4 emissions from storage would be necessary. There have been some efforts to achieve this (USEPA Agstar, 2010), but these are not common practice. Other opportunities for mitigation of the environmental impacts are reduction of NH3 emissions from the housing (Jacobson, 2011) and storage (USEPA Agstar, 2010). Nitrous oxide emissions from the field may be mitigated by adding biochar to storage units or applying it with fertilizers (Steiner et al., 2010; Taghizadeh-Toosi, 2011; Taghizadeh-Toosi et al., 2012). A better understanding of the impact of soil conditions, application timing, and rate may yield N2 emission reductions as well.

In this LCA, we compared manure with synthetic fertilizer as a nutrient source for CC and CS rotations. When manure treatment techniques are compared, the functional unit is often disposal of a fixed amount of manure. The system is then expanded to calculate credits for displaced synthetic fertilizer use. This functional unit, which allows one to compare the impact of treatment of a fixed amount of manure, was the approach used by Griffing et al. (2004) and Lopez-Ridaura et al. (2009). In both studies, the manure was assumed to displace synthetic fertilizer macronutrients on a one-to-one basis. A similar approach was

Fig. 8. Scenario analysis of global warming potential (GWP) emissions per ton of corn. AA, allocate all emissions from NH2 production to NH2; DP, deep pit; IPCC CH4 use Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change emission factor for CH4 emissions from the deep pit; S, synthetic fertilizer.
taken by Ogino et al. (2012) when comparing alternative uses of concentrated rice-washing water.

When doing an LCA of crop production and comparing synthetic fertilizers with manure, the result depends on the crop macronutrient needs. Thus, the best functional unit is crop production, which was used in this article and by Cerutti et al. (2011). This functional unit forces the LCA to handle nutrient imbalances in the same manner as farm managers. It also allows for a clear illustration of the impacts of various synthetic fertilizers in the absence of manure. In the corn fertilization case in this LCA, we assumed that all of the corn N needs would be met by N in the manure. Some excess P was assumed to build up in the soil over time. In future years, this P would eventually displace synthetic production, but no credit was applied. The amount of excess was small for DP storage and may present a significant issue with ALs. In the CS rotation, the total land area needed per unit hog was much greater (Table 6). When the N need of the crops was again supplied by manure, the P needs were not met, and we assumed that these were supplemented by synthetic fertilizer. From an LCA perspective, the underutilization of P in the absence of soybean was not an important factor. This is illustrated by the relative magnitude of impacts from P and N from synthetic fertilization (Fig. 4b). Fertilization of P has about 5 to 20% of the impact of N fertilization. In CC rotations with lagoon storage, about half of the P in swine waste was not utilized. Thus, the potential savings was about 3 to 10% of the impact from synthetic fertilizers. However, from a long-term management perspective, fertilization of a purely corn rotation with anaerobic lagoon treatment would lead to unsustainable P buildup. Because of the large emissions during housing and storage of manure, the choice of LCA boundary and the choice of whether or not to assign of a credit for alternative disposal method are important to the comparison of manure to synthetic fertilizer. In this LCA, no credit was assigned. Thus, emissions were assigned to the corn crop instead of the pork. If a credit were assigned for alternative disposal of swine manure, the environmental burdens would shift from the manured corn to the pork. When doing an LCA on animal production, it is best to include the emissions from housing and storage and use system expansion to include a credit for avoided synthetic fertilizer use. This system avoids allocation as per ISO 14044. However, it precludes a direct
comparison of the two fertilizer systems and was not suitable for this comparison.
63: The case study farm grew hogs from nursery to full size over 165 d. Each year, two of these growth cycles were completed. Representative daily excretion rates from Table 1 multiplied by 330 d result in yearly excretions rates of 201 g N LW and 30 g P kg- LW. If applied directly, this nutrient production per kg LW is sufficient to fertilize CC or CS at rates specified in Table 5. In a CC rotation, all application bases resulted in a comparable amount of live weight per hectare. In the CS rotation, the N application rates were less than half that of the CC rotation. Thus, a larger amount of cropland is required per hog. In this case, application of manure on a P basis would result in an excess of N applied.

Table 6 shows crop area fertilized per 1000 hog population for as excreted, DP, and AL scenarios. A population of 1000 hogs is consistent with a production rate of 2000 hogs per year. For the continuous corn rotation with the DP and AL storage methods, the P basis resulted in more area fertilized per hog. Thus, application on an N basis would result in an excess of P applied. The DP storage resulted in manure that best matched application guidelines. For the CS rotation, the crop needs a higher amount of P, relative to N. Thus, the AL storage method results in manure that best matches the application rate guidelines.
The three most common recommended rates for fertilizer application in Iowa and the balance of nutrient application and

plant uptake are summarized in Table 7. Nutrient uptake values include only nutrients leaving the field in the grain. Using the MRTN rate of application and factoring in biological N fixation from soybean, over a 2-yr rotation of CS, the total N input was 339 kg N ha-Superscript(1) (138 kg N ha from manure and 201 kg N ha-Superscript(1) from biological N fixation), and the uptake was 359 kg N ha-1, leaving an apparent net N deficit of N of 20 kg N ha-1. Mineralization of soil organic N contributes to the N balance. In general, about 3 to 4% of soil organic N and 50% of crop residue can be mineralized in given year, which equates to a 156 kg N ha-1 "credit" allowed for corn following soybean. The application rates used in this LCA for CC and CS systems are specified in Table 7.

to the fields are nearly equal to the requirements for housing, pumping, and spreading combined. However, from a global warming perspective, the impact of transport was small relative to the impact of manure CH and N2O emissions from storage and the field. In long-distance transport situations, the optimum yield economics are altered, and there may be an incentive to overapply manure. Overapplication could also lead to increased eutrophication, acidification, and GWP. Thus, the economic and ecologic optimum application rates may diverge for regions of high hog population.

For synthetic fertilizer (4b), the largest contributions to global warming and eutrophication impact were during land application (N2O emissions and NO leaching). In other categories, particularly acidification, the majority of emissions are from N fertilizer production (UAN). Triple superphosphate production contributes about 10 to 20% in many impact categories. Seventy-five percent of the fossil fuel consumption and abiotic depletion associated with TSP production was from sulfur, which is a byproduct of oil refining.

The synthetic fertilizer was compared with manure (DP and AL storage) for a CC rotation (Fig. 5). Error bars in Fig. 5 represent the 95% interval from Monte Carlo simulations. Global warming impacts for synthetic fertilization were the

Fig. Global warming potential (GWP) by chemical emission and production step for deep pit storage with 24-km long distance transport (LDT) to field. Field, field emissions; HD, housing direct emissions; HE, housing energy emissions.
lowest. When storage systems were compared, the DP system had 50% less GWP than the AL system. This comparison was due to reduction in CH emissions from the storage system and conservation of N.

The acidification and eutrophication impacts, which were strongly influenced by NH3 emissions from housing, are significantly lower for DP than for AL. The DP was assumed to have NH, emissions of 18% (15-25%), versus 37% (23-51%) for the AL. These improvements were amplified by the conservation of N. On a unit crop production basis, more live weight was required for the AL than for the DP, as shown by the live weight category. Consequently, the amount of N treated and associated acidification, eutrophication, GWP, and photochemical oxidation impacts were higher. The live weight needed was 57 + 15 kg d LW kg- corn for AL and 36 + 8 kg d LW kg-1 corn for DP. In Fig. 5, the error bars for live weight reflect relatively large uncertainties in the swine waste characterization data (N production per unit live weight) as well as the emissions of N in storage. However, variations in swine waste characteristics do not affect the comparison between storage methods. Thus, the error bars here are indicative of the certainty between manure and synthetic fertilizer and not between storage methods.

On a unit product basis, however, the N applied in the CS rotation is much lower and contributes to lower GWP emissions. In the CS rotation, GWP emissions were 1.26, 0.58, and 0.19 kg CO2e (kg corn plus 0.298 kg soybean) for the AL, DP, and synthetic fertilizers, respectively. Important LCA impact categories in manure and synthetic fertilization are fossil energy consumption, GWP, eutrophication, and acidification (Reckmann et al., 2012). Figure 7 combines information from Fig. 4 and 5 for important categories of the life cycle analysis for the continuous corn rotation. For each nutrient source, Fig. 7 shows the relative size of the emissions by stage, which can be used to estimate the contribution of each emission to the overall uncertainty in Fig. 5 and 6.

Eutrophication from synthetic fertilization was dominated by NO leaching from the field (80%) and cradle-to-gate production (20%). Eutrophication from the manure fertilization was dominated by NH emissions from housing/storage (60%) and field emissions (40%). Leaching of N was estimated to be 24% of total synthetic N (Lawlor et al., 2008) applied and 9.6% of total organic N applied (Gupta et al., 2004). Nitrogen in manure is considered to be released slower than that UAN, and this can lead to lower expected leaching. The higher leaching rate for synthetic fertilizers from the field roughly made up for the eutrophication impact of NH emissions from housing/storage. Given the large variability in these data, eutrophication potential is equally affected by manures and synthetic fertilizers.

Acidification was dominated by NH emissions from housing/storage. These emissions represented 97% of the total acidification for the DP. The acidification emissions for synthetic fertilizer were about 11% of those for the DP case. These were dominated by energy-related emissions in the production of UAN.

The life cycle impacts of abiotic depletion (depletion of nonliving natural resources) and nonrenewable fossil energy use are nearly identical in this analysis. For synthetic fertilizer, these are dominated by cradle-to-gate production. Nitrogen fertilizer uses the largest amount of fuel, and P-based fertilizers contribute about 20%. The N fertilizer fossil inputs were due to natural gas use, and the P fuel use was due to consumption of sulfur, which

Fig. 7. Emissions per process stage for important impact categories. Acid, acidification; AD, abiotic depletion; AL, anaerobic lagoon; CTG, cradle to gate; DP, deep pit; Eutroph, eutrophication; Fossil, fossil fuel depletion; FW ecotox, freshwater ecotoxicity; GWP, global warming potential; H tox, human toxicity; MA ecotox, marine aquatic ecotoxicity; ODP, ozone depletion; Photo Ox, photochemical oxidation; S, synthetic fertilizer; T ecotox, terrestrial ecotoxicity.
is a byproduct of oil production. Although P consumption has been identified as an important nonrenewable resource, it does not show up in the CML method.

The GWP is an important impact category in this study, and there were several decisions in the methodology and life cycle inventory that affected the overall comparison. The Iowa representative CH emission factor from the DP was assumed to be larger than the IPCC tier 2 value. The N2O emissions from the field were assumed to be higher for manure than for UAN application. However, these emissions are considered to be equal in the IPCC methodology. In addition, some of the emissions in the NH3 production process were allocated to CO2. In the comparison, the allocation factor was allowed to vary, SO that the impact of this choice was included in the error bars. All of these choices increased the GWP of manure relative to synthetic fertilizer. The impact of these methodological and impact factor choices on GWP is shown in Fig. 8. For UAN, the allocation was set all to NH3 or allowed to vary between all to NH3 and mass allocation. For the DP case, the CH, emissions were set to the Iowa representative value of 0.7 g kg-1 LW d-¹ or the IPCC value of 0.35 g kg- LW d-¹. For each of these cases, the N2O emissions from the field were set to be equal as described by the IPCC or set to the Iowa representative values (Jarecki et al., 2008; Chantigny et al., 2010). Setting the CH4 emission factor equal to the IPCC value resulted in a large reduction in GWP for the DP case. Changing the allocation method in synthetic fertilizer production resulted in a significant change when using the IPCC N20 emission factor (1.0%). However, the relative change is less with the Iowa representative N,O emissions (Jarecki et al., 2008; Chantigny et al., 2010). This change was also small relative to the difference between the DP and synthetic emission factors. Changing the N2O emission factors from the Iowa representative and IPCC values made a significant difference. However, the comparison between the DP and synthetic fertilizer is not materially changed by this choice. Under all of the scenarios considered, synthetic fertilizer showed significantly lower emissions than the stored manure.

In order for manure to be competitive with synthetic fertilizer with respect to GWP, a reduction in CH4 emissions from storage would be necessary. There have been some efforts to achieve this (USEPA Agstar, 2010), but these are not common practice. Other opportunities for mitigation of the environmental impacts are reduction of NH3 emissions from the housing (Jacobson, 2011) and storage (USEPA Agstar, 2010). Nitrous oxide emissions from the field may be mitigated by adding biochar to storage units or applying it with fertilizers (Steiner et al., 2010; Taghizadeh-Toosi, 2011; Taghizadeh-Toosi et al., 2012). A better understanding of the impact of soil conditions, application timing, and rate may yield N2 emission reductions as well.

In this LCA, we compared manure with synthetic fertilizer as a nutrient source for CC and CS rotations. When manure treatment techniques are compared, the functional unit is often disposal of a fixed amount of manure. The system is then expanded to calculate credits for displaced synthetic fertilizer use. This functional unit, which allows one to compare the impact of treatment of a fixed amount of manure, was the approach used by Griffing et al. (2004) and Lopez-Ridaura et al. (2009). In both studies, the manure was assumed to displace synthetic fertilizer macronutrients on a one-to-one basis. A similar approach was

Fig. 8. Scenario analysis of global warming potential (GWP) emissions per ton of corn. AA, allocate all emissions from NH2 production to NH2; DP, deep pit; IPCC CH4 use Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change emission factor for CH4 emissions from the deep pit; S, synthetic fertilizer.
taken by Ogino et al. (2012) when comparing alternative uses of concentrated rice-washing water.

When doing an LCA of crop production and comparing synthetic fertilizers with manure, the result depends on the crop macronutrient needs. Thus, the best functional unit is crop production, which was used in this article and by Cerutti et al. (2011). This functional unit forces the LCA to handle nutrient imbalances in the same manner as farm managers. It also allows for a clear illustration of the impacts of various synthetic fertilizers in the absence of manure. In the corn fertilization case in this LCA, we assumed that all of the corn N needs would be met by N in the manure. Some excess P was assumed to build up in the soil over time. In future years, this P would eventually displace synthetic production, but no credit was applied. The amount of excess was small for DP storage and may present a significant issue with ALs. In the CS rotation, the total land area needed per unit hog was much greater (Table 6). When the N need of the crops was again supplied by manure, the P needs were not met, and we assumed that these were supplemented by synthetic fertilizer. From an LCA perspective, the underutilization of P in the absence of soybean was not an important factor. This is illustrated by the relative magnitude of impacts from P and N from synthetic fertilization (Fig. 4b). Fertilization of P has about 5 to 20% of the impact of N fertilization. In CC rotations with lagoon storage, about half of the P in swine waste was not utilized. Thus, the potential savings was about 3 to 10% of the impact from synthetic fertilizers. However, from a long-term management perspective, fertilization of a purely corn rotation with anaerobic lagoon treatment would lead to unsustainable P buildup. Because of the large emissions during housing and storage of manure, the choice of LCA boundary and the choice of whether or not to assign of a credit for alternative disposal method are important to the comparison of manure to synthetic fertilizer. In this LCA, no credit was assigned. Thus, emissions were assigned to the corn crop instead of the pork. If a credit were assigned for alternative disposal of swine manure, the environmental burdens would shift from the manured corn to the pork. When doing an LCA on animal production, it is best to include the emissions from housing and storage and use system expansion to include a credit for avoided synthetic fertilizer use. This system avoids allocation as per ISO 14044. However, it precludes a direct
comparison of the two fertilizer systems and was not suitable for this comparison.
64: The case study farm grew hogs from nursery to full size over 165 d. Each year, two of these growth cycles were completed. Representative daily excretion rates from Table 1 multiplied by 330 d result in yearly excretions rates of 201 g N LW and 30 g P kg- LW. If applied directly, this nutrient production per kg LW is sufficient to fertilize CC or CS at rates specified in Table 5. In a CC rotation, all application bases resulted in a comparable amount of live weight per hectare. In the CS rotation, the N application rates were less than half that of the CC rotation. Thus, a larger amount of cropland is required per hog. In this case, application of manure on a P basis would result in an excess of N applied.

Table 6 shows crop area fertilized per 1000 hog population for as excreted, DP, and AL scenarios. A population of 1000 hogs is consistent with a production rate of 2000 hogs per year. For the continuous corn rotation with the DP and AL storage methods, the P basis resulted in more area fertilized per hog. Thus, application on an N basis would result in an excess of P applied. The DP storage resulted in manure that best matched application guidelines. For the CS rotation, the crop needs a higher amount of P, relative to N. Thus, the AL storage method results in manure that best matches the application rate guidelines.
The three most common recommended rates for fertilizer application in Iowa and the balance of nutrient application and

plant uptake are summarized in Table 7. Nutrient uptake values include only nutrients leaving the field in the grain. Using the MRTN rate of application and factoring in biological N fixation from soybean, over a 2-yr rotation of CS, the total N input was 339 kg N ha-Superscript(1) (138 kg N ha from manure and 201 kg N ha-Superscript(1) from biological N fixation), and the uptake was 359 kg N ha-1, leaving an apparent net N deficit of N of 20 kg N ha-1. Mineralization of soil organic N contributes to the N balance. In general, about 3 to 4% of soil organic N and 50% of crop residue can be mineralized in given year, which equates to a 156 kg N ha-1 "credit" allowed for corn following soybean. The application rates used in this LCA for CC and CS systems are specified in Table 7.

Matching nutrients in manure with crop needs is important for efficient utilization of nutrients and for prevention of P build-up in soil. The best crop rotation for nutrient matching depends on residual P levels in the soil, manure analysis, and storage method. Manure in concert with synthetic fertilizers and crop rotation is effective, economical, and low risk if storage and application follow best management practices and soil nutrient assays coupled with manure nutrient composition are used to determine rates of application. This method is common practice in Iowa and was used on the case study farm.

The hog population of Iowa is approximately 20 million market hogs and breeders. Using best management practices and amending with synthetic fertilizers for a CS rotation, these hogs produce enough manure to fertilize 3.24 million ha (DP, MRTN) or 1.62 million ha (DP, P index) of Iowa's total farm acreage, which is approximately 12.5 million ha. Thus, on a state-wide basis, hog manure can be efficiently used with a small fraction of the total available land. A similar analysis including other types of livestock and crops would provide a more complete picture of the nutrient balance in Iowa.

A Monte Carlo analysis of CTG GWP of NH3 is given in Fig. 2. The three cases shown are (i) the Environmental Clarity model with the allocation parameter set to 0.5 (allocated 50% by mass and 50% all to NH3), (ii) The Environmental Clarity model with the allocation parameter allowed to vary from 0 to 1 (allocated all to mass or all to NH3), and (iii) ecoinvent module "ammonia, steam reforming, liquid, at plant/RER." The first and third models have similar GWPs (1.7 and 1.9 kg CO2 kg NH, respectively). The 95% confidence intervals were +15 to 20%. The second model, in which the allocation parameter varies,

Important LCA impact categories in manure and synthetic fertilization are fossil energy consumption, GWP, eutrophication, and acidification (Reckmann et al., 2012). Figure 7 combines information from Fig. 4 and 5 for important categories of the life cycle analysis for the continuous corn rotation. For each nutrient source, Fig. 7 shows the relative size of the emissions by stage, which can be used to estimate the contribution of each emission to the overall uncertainty in Fig. 5 and 6.

Eutrophication from synthetic fertilization was dominated by NO leaching from the field (80%) and cradle-to-gate production (20%). Eutrophication from the manure fertilization was dominated by NH emissions from housing/storage (60%) and field emissions (40%). Leaching of N was estimated to be 24% of total synthetic N (Lawlor et al., 2008) applied and 9.6% of total organic N applied (Gupta et al., 2004). Nitrogen in manure is considered to be released slower than that UAN, and this can lead to lower expected leaching. The higher leaching rate for synthetic fertilizers from the field roughly made up for the eutrophication impact of NH emissions from housing/storage. Given the large variability in these data, eutrophication potential is equally affected by manures and synthetic fertilizers.

Acidification was dominated by NH emissions from housing/storage. These emissions represented 97% of the total acidification for the DP. The acidification emissions for synthetic fertilizer were about 11% of those for the DP case. These were dominated by energy-related emissions in the production of UAN.

The life cycle impacts of abiotic depletion (depletion of nonliving natural resources) and nonrenewable fossil energy use are nearly identical in this analysis. For synthetic fertilizer, these are dominated by cradle-to-gate production. Nitrogen fertilizer uses the largest amount of fuel, and P-based fertilizers contribute about 20%. The N fertilizer fossil inputs were due to natural gas use, and the P fuel use was due to consumption of sulfur, which

Fig. 7. Emissions per process stage for important impact categories. Acid, acidification; AD, abiotic depletion; AL, anaerobic lagoon; CTG, cradle to gate; DP, deep pit; Eutroph, eutrophication; Fossil, fossil fuel depletion; FW ecotox, freshwater ecotoxicity; GWP, global warming potential; H tox, human toxicity; MA ecotox, marine aquatic ecotoxicity; ODP, ozone depletion; Photo Ox, photochemical oxidation; S, synthetic fertilizer; T ecotox, terrestrial ecotoxicity.
is a byproduct of oil production. Although P consumption has been identified as an important nonrenewable resource, it does not show up in the CML method.

The GWP is an important impact category in this study, and there were several decisions in the methodology and life cycle inventory that affected the overall comparison. The Iowa representative CH emission factor from the DP was assumed to be larger than the IPCC tier 2 value. The N2O emissions from the field were assumed to be higher for manure than for UAN application. However, these emissions are considered to be equal in the IPCC methodology. In addition, some of the emissions in the NH3 production process were allocated to CO2. In the comparison, the allocation factor was allowed to vary, SO that the impact of this choice was included in the error bars. All of these choices increased the GWP of manure relative to synthetic fertilizer. The impact of these methodological and impact factor choices on GWP is shown in Fig. 8. For UAN, the allocation was set all to NH3 or allowed to vary between all to NH3 and mass allocation. For the DP case, the CH, emissions were set to the Iowa representative value of 0.7 g kg-1 LW d-¹ or the IPCC value of 0.35 g kg- LW d-¹. For each of these cases, the N2O emissions from the field were set to be equal as described by the IPCC or set to the Iowa representative values (Jarecki et al., 2008; Chantigny et al., 2010). Setting the CH4 emission factor equal to the IPCC value resulted in a large reduction in GWP for the DP case. Changing the allocation method in synthetic fertilizer production resulted in a significant change when using the IPCC N20 emission factor (1.0%). However, the relative change is less with the Iowa representative N,O emissions (Jarecki et al., 2008; Chantigny et al., 2010). This change was also small relative to the difference between the DP and synthetic emission factors. Changing the N2O emission factors from the Iowa representative and IPCC values made a significant difference. However, the comparison between the DP and synthetic fertilizer is not materially changed by this choice. Under all of the scenarios considered, synthetic fertilizer showed significantly lower emissions than the stored manure.

In order for manure to be competitive with synthetic fertilizer with respect to GWP, a reduction in CH4 emissions from storage would be necessary. There have been some efforts to achieve this (USEPA Agstar, 2010), but these are not common practice. Other opportunities for mitigation of the environmental impacts are reduction of NH3 emissions from the housing (Jacobson, 2011) and storage (USEPA Agstar, 2010). Nitrous oxide emissions from the field may be mitigated by adding biochar to storage units or applying it with fertilizers (Steiner et al., 2010; Taghizadeh-Toosi, 2011; Taghizadeh-Toosi et al., 2012). A better understanding of the impact of soil conditions, application timing, and rate may yield N2 emission reductions as well.

In this LCA, we compared manure with synthetic fertilizer as a nutrient source for CC and CS rotations. When manure treatment techniques are compared, the functional unit is often disposal of a fixed amount of manure. The system is then expanded to calculate credits for displaced synthetic fertilizer use. This functional unit, which allows one to compare the impact of treatment of a fixed amount of manure, was the approach used by Griffing et al. (2004) and Lopez-Ridaura et al. (2009). In both studies, the manure was assumed to displace synthetic fertilizer macronutrients on a one-to-one basis. A similar approach was

Fig. 8. Scenario analysis of global warming potential (GWP) emissions per ton of corn. AA, allocate all emissions from NH2 production to NH2; DP, deep pit; IPCC CH4 use Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change emission factor for CH4 emissions from the deep pit; S, synthetic fertilizer.
taken by Ogino et al. (2012) when comparing alternative uses of concentrated rice-washing water.

When doing an LCA of crop production and comparing synthetic fertilizers with manure, the result depends on the crop macronutrient needs. Thus, the best functional unit is crop production, which was used in this article and by Cerutti et al. (2011). This functional unit forces the LCA to handle nutrient imbalances in the same manner as farm managers. It also allows for a clear illustration of the impacts of various synthetic fertilizers in the absence of manure. In the corn fertilization case in this LCA, we assumed that all of the corn N needs would be met by N in the manure. Some excess P was assumed to build up in the soil over time. In future years, this P would eventually displace synthetic production, but no credit was applied. The amount of excess was small for DP storage and may present a significant issue with ALs. In the CS rotation, the total land area needed per unit hog was much greater (Table 6). When the N need of the crops was again supplied by manure, the P needs were not met, and we assumed that these were supplemented by synthetic fertilizer. From an LCA perspective, the underutilization of P in the absence of soybean was not an important factor. This is illustrated by the relative magnitude of impacts from P and N from synthetic fertilization (Fig. 4b). Fertilization of P has about 5 to 20% of the impact of N fertilization. In CC rotations with lagoon storage, about half of the P in swine waste was not utilized. Thus, the potential savings was about 3 to 10% of the impact from synthetic fertilizers. However, from a long-term management perspective, fertilization of a purely corn rotation with anaerobic lagoon treatment would lead to unsustainable P buildup. Because of the large emissions during housing and storage of manure, the choice of LCA boundary and the choice of whether or not to assign of a credit for alternative disposal method are important to the comparison of manure to synthetic fertilizer. In this LCA, no credit was assigned. Thus, emissions were assigned to the corn crop instead of the pork. If a credit were assigned for alternative disposal of swine manure, the environmental burdens would shift from the manured corn to the pork. When doing an LCA on animal production, it is best to include the emissions from housing and storage and use system expansion to include a credit for avoided synthetic fertilizer use. This system avoids allocation as per ISO 14044. However, it precludes a direct
comparison of the two fertilizer systems and was not suitable for this comparison.
65: The case study farm grew hogs from nursery to full size over 165 d. Each year, two of these growth cycles were completed. Representative daily excretion rates from Table 1 multiplied by 330 d result in yearly excretions rates of 201 g N LW and 30 g P kg- LW. If applied directly, this nutrient production per kg LW is sufficient to fertilize CC or CS at rates specified in Table 5. In a CC rotation, all application bases resulted in a comparable amount of live weight per hectare. In the CS rotation, the N application rates were less than half that of the CC rotation. Thus, a larger amount of cropland is required per hog. In this case, application of manure on a P basis would result in an excess of N applied.

Table 6 shows crop area fertilized per 1000 hog population for as excreted, DP, and AL scenarios. A population of 1000 hogs is consistent with a production rate of 2000 hogs per year. For the continuous corn rotation with the DP and AL storage methods, the P basis resulted in more area fertilized per hog. Thus, application on an N basis would result in an excess of P applied. The DP storage resulted in manure that best matched application guidelines. For the CS rotation, the crop needs a higher amount of P, relative to N. Thus, the AL storage method results in manure that best matches the application rate guidelines.
The three most common recommended rates for fertilizer application in Iowa and the balance of nutrient application and

plant uptake are summarized in Table 7. Nutrient uptake values include only nutrients leaving the field in the grain. Using the MRTN rate of application and factoring in biological N fixation from soybean, over a 2-yr rotation of CS, the total N input was 339 kg N ha-Superscript(1) (138 kg N ha from manure and 201 kg N ha-Superscript(1) from biological N fixation), and the uptake was 359 kg N ha-1, leaving an apparent net N deficit of N of 20 kg N ha-1. Mineralization of soil organic N contributes to the N balance. In general, about 3 to 4% of soil organic N and 50% of crop residue can be mineralized in given year, which equates to a 156 kg N ha-1 "credit" allowed for corn following soybean. The application rates used in this LCA for CC and CS systems are specified in Table 7.

Matching nutrients in manure with crop needs is important for efficient utilization of nutrients and for prevention of P build-up in soil. The best crop rotation for nutrient matching depends on residual P levels in the soil, manure analysis, and storage method. Manure in concert with synthetic fertilizers and crop rotation is effective, economical, and low risk if storage and application follow best management practices and soil nutrient assays coupled with manure nutrient composition are used to determine rates of application. This method is common practice in Iowa and was used on the case study farm.

The hog population of Iowa is approximately 20 million market hogs and breeders. Using best management practices and amending with synthetic fertilizers for a CS rotation, these hogs produce enough manure to fertilize 3.24 million ha (DP, MRTN) or 1.62 million ha (DP, P index) of Iowa's total farm acreage, which is approximately 12.5 million ha. Thus, on a state-wide basis, hog manure can be efficiently used with a small fraction of the total available land. A similar analysis including other types of livestock and crops would provide a more complete picture of the nutrient balance in Iowa.

However, from a global warming perspective, the impact of transport was small relative to the impact of manure CH and N2O emissions from storage and the field. In long-distance transport situations, the optimum yield economics are altered, and there may be an incentive to overapply manure. Overapplication could also lead to increased eutrophication, acidification, and GWP. Thus, the economic and ecologic optimum application rates may diverge for regions of high hog population.

For synthetic fertilizer (4b), the largest contributions to global warming and eutrophication impact were during land application (N2O emissions and NO leaching). In other categories, particularly acidification, the majority of emissions are from N fertilizer production (UAN). Triple superphosphate production contributes about 10 to 20% in many impact categories. Seventy-five percent of the fossil fuel consumption and abiotic depletion associated with TSP production was from sulfur, which is a byproduct of oil refining.

The synthetic fertilizer was compared with manure (DP and AL storage) for a CC rotation (Fig. 5). Error bars in Fig. 5 represent the 95% interval from Monte Carlo simulations. Global warming impacts for synthetic fertilization were the

Fig. Global warming potential (GWP) by chemical emission and production step for deep pit storage with 24-km long distance transport (LDT) to field. Field, field emissions; HD, housing direct emissions; HE, housing energy emissions.
lowest. When storage systems were compared, the DP system had 50% less GWP than the AL system. This comparison was due to reduction in CH emissions from the storage system and conservation of N.

The acidification and eutrophication impacts, which were strongly influenced by NH3 emissions from housing, are significantly lower for DP than for AL. The DP was assumed to have NH, emissions of 18% (15-25%), versus 37% (23-51%) for the AL. These improvements were amplified by the conservation of N. On a unit crop production basis, more live weight was required for the AL than for the DP, as shown by the live weight category. Consequently, the amount of N treated and associated acidification, eutrophication, GWP, and photochemical oxidation impacts were higher. The live weight needed was 57 + 15 kg d LW kg- corn for AL and 36 + 8 kg d LW kg-1 corn for DP. In Fig. 5, the error bars for live weight reflect relatively large uncertainties in the swine waste characterization data (N production per unit live weight) as well as the emissions of N in storage. However, variations in swine waste characteristics do not affect the comparison between storage methods. Thus, the error bars here are indicative of the certainty between manure and synthetic fertilizer and not between storage methods.

In this LCA, no credit was assigned. Thus, emissions were assigned to the corn crop instead of the pork. If a credit were assigned for alternative disposal of swine manure, the environmental burdens would shift from the manured corn to the pork. When doing an LCA on animal production, it is best to include the emissions from housing and storage and use system expansion to include a credit for avoided synthetic fertilizer use. This system avoids allocation as per ISO 14044. However, it precludes a direct
comparison of the two fertilizer systems and was not suitable for this comparison.
66: The case study farm grew hogs from nursery to full size over 165 d. Each year, two of these growth cycles were completed. Representative daily excretion rates from Table 1 multiplied by 330 d result in yearly excretions rates of 201 g N LW and 30 g P kg- LW. If applied directly, this nutrient production per kg LW is sufficient to fertilize CC or CS at rates specified in Table 5. In a CC rotation, all application bases resulted in a comparable amount of live weight per hectare. In the CS rotation, the N application rates were less than half that of the CC rotation. Thus, a larger amount of cropland is required per hog. In this case, application of manure on a P basis would result in an excess of N applied.

Table 6 shows crop area fertilized per 1000 hog population for as excreted, DP, and AL scenarios. A population of 1000 hogs is consistent with a production rate of 2000 hogs per year. For the continuous corn rotation with the DP and AL storage methods, the P basis resulted in more area fertilized per hog. Thus, application on an N basis would result in an excess of P applied. The DP storage resulted in manure that best matched application guidelines. For the CS rotation, the crop needs a higher amount of P, relative to N. Thus, the AL storage method results in manure that best matches the application rate guidelines.
The three most common recommended rates for fertilizer application in Iowa and the balance of nutrient application and

plant uptake are summarized in Table 7. Nutrient uptake values include only nutrients leaving the field in the grain. Using the MRTN rate of application and factoring in biological N fixation from soybean, over a 2-yr rotation of CS, the total N input was 339 kg N ha-Superscript(1) (138 kg N ha from manure and 201 kg N ha-Superscript(1) from biological N fixation), and the uptake was 359 kg N ha-1, leaving an apparent net N deficit of N of 20 kg N ha-1. Mineralization of soil organic N contributes to the N balance. In general, about 3 to 4% of soil organic N and 50% of crop residue can be mineralized in given year, which equates to a 156 kg N ha-1 "credit" allowed for corn following soybean. The application rates used in this LCA for CC and CS systems are specified in Table 7.

Matching nutrients in manure with crop needs is important for efficient utilization of nutrients and for prevention of P build-up in soil. The best crop rotation for nutrient matching depends on residual P levels in the soil, manure analysis, and storage method. Manure in concert with synthetic fertilizers and crop rotation is effective, economical, and low risk if storage and application follow best management practices and soil nutrient assays coupled with manure nutrient composition are used to determine rates of application. This method is common practice in Iowa and was used on the case study farm.

The hog population of Iowa is approximately 20 million market hogs and breeders. Using best management practices and amending with synthetic fertilizers for a CS rotation, these hogs produce enough manure to fertilize 3.24 million ha (DP, MRTN) or 1.62 million ha (DP, P index) of Iowa's total farm acreage, which is approximately 12.5 million ha. Thus, on a state-wide basis, hog manure can be efficiently used with a small fraction of the total available land. A similar analysis including other types of livestock and crops would provide a more complete picture of the nutrient balance in Iowa.

However, from a global warming perspective, the impact of transport was small relative to the impact of manure CH and N2O emissions from storage and the field. In long-distance transport situations, the optimum yield economics are altered, and there may be an incentive to overapply manure. Overapplication could also lead to increased eutrophication, acidification, and GWP. Thus, the economic and ecologic optimum application rates may diverge for regions of high hog population.

For synthetic fertilizer (4b), the largest contributions to global warming and eutrophication impact were during land application (N2O emissions and NO leaching). In other categories, particularly acidification, the majority of emissions are from N fertilizer production (UAN). Triple superphosphate production contributes about 10 to 20% in many impact categories. Seventy-five percent of the fossil fuel consumption and abiotic depletion associated with TSP production was from sulfur, which is a byproduct of oil refining.

The synthetic fertilizer was compared with manure (DP and AL storage) for a CC rotation (Fig. 5). Error bars in Fig. 5 represent the 95% interval from Monte Carlo simulations. Global warming impacts for synthetic fertilization were the

Fig. Global warming potential (GWP) by chemical emission and production step for deep pit storage with 24-km long distance transport (LDT) to field. Field, field emissions; HD, housing direct emissions; HE, housing energy emissions.
lowest. When storage systems were compared, the DP system had 50% less GWP than the AL system. This comparison was due to reduction in CH emissions from the storage system and conservation of N.

The acidification and eutrophication impacts, which were strongly influenced by NH3 emissions from housing, are significantly lower for DP than for AL. The DP was assumed to have NH, emissions of 18% (15-25%), versus 37% (23-51%) for the AL. These improvements were amplified by the conservation of N. On a unit crop production basis, more live weight was required for the AL than for the DP, as shown by the live weight category. Consequently, the amount of N treated and associated acidification, eutrophication, GWP, and photochemical oxidation impacts were higher. The live weight needed was 57 + 15 kg d LW kg- corn for AL and 36 + 8 kg d LW kg-1 corn for DP. In Fig. 5, the error bars for live weight reflect relatively large uncertainties in the swine waste characterization data (N production per unit live weight) as well as the emissions of N in storage. However, variations in swine waste characteristics do not affect the comparison between storage methods. Thus, the error bars here are indicative of the certainty between manure and synthetic fertilizer and not between storage methods.

On a unit product basis, however, the N applied in the CS rotation is much lower and contributes to lower GWP emissions. In the CS rotation, GWP emissions were 1.26, 0.58, and 0.19 kg CO2e (kg corn plus 0.298 kg soybean) for the AL, DP, and synthetic fertilizers, respectively. The addition of soybean lowers the net fertilization emission due to the N credit produced by fixation. This compares with an emission of 0.2 kg CO kg-1 corn in the 2012 GREET model

Important LCA impact categories in manure and synthetic fertilization are fossil energy consumption, GWP, eutrophication, and acidification (Reckmann et al., 2012). Figure 7 combines information from Fig. 4 and 5 for important categories of the life cycle analysis for the continuous corn rotation. For each nutrient source, Fig. 7 shows the relative size of the emissions by stage, which can be used to estimate the contribution of each emission to the overall uncertainty in Fig. 5 and 6.

Eutrophication from synthetic fertilization was dominated by NO leaching from the field (80%) and cradle-to-gate production (20%). Eutrophication from the manure fertilization was dominated by NH emissions from housing/storage (60%) and field emissions (40%). Leaching of N was estimated to be 24% of total synthetic N (Lawlor et al., 2008) applied and 9.6% of total organic N applied (Gupta et al., 2004). Nitrogen in manure is considered to be released slower than that UAN, and this can lead to lower expected leaching. The higher leaching rate for synthetic fertilizers from the field roughly made up for the eutrophication impact of NH emissions from housing/storage. Given the large variability in these data, eutrophication potential is equally affected by manures and synthetic fertilizers.

Acidification was dominated by NH emissions from housing/storage. These emissions represented 97% of the total acidification for the DP. The acidification emissions for synthetic fertilizer were about 11% of those for the DP case. These were dominated by energy-related emissions in the production of UAN.

The life cycle impacts of abiotic depletion (depletion of nonliving natural resources) and nonrenewable fossil energy use are nearly identical in this analysis. For synthetic fertilizer, these are dominated by cradle-to-gate production. Nitrogen fertilizer uses the largest amount of fuel, and P-based fertilizers contribute about 20%. The N fertilizer fossil inputs were due to natural gas use, and the P fuel use was due to consumption of sulfur, which

Fig. 7. Emissions per process stage for important impact categories. is a byproduct of oil production. Although P consumption has been identified as an important nonrenewable resource, it does not show up in the CML method.

The GWP is an important impact category in this study, and there were several decisions in the methodology and life cycle inventory that affected the overall comparison. The Iowa representative CH emission factor from the DP was assumed to be larger than the IPCC tier 2 value. The N2O emissions from the field were assumed to be higher for manure than for UAN application. However, these emissions are considered to be equal in the IPCC methodology. In addition, some of the emissions in the NH3 production process were allocated to CO2. In the comparison, the allocation factor was allowed to vary, SO that the impact of this choice was included in the error bars. All of these choices increased the GWP of manure relative to synthetic fertilizer. The impact of these methodological and impact factor choices on GWP is shown in Fig. 8. For UAN, the allocation was set all to NH3 or allowed to vary between all to NH3 and mass allocation. For the DP case, the CH, emissions were set to the Iowa representative value of 0.7 g kg-1 LW d-¹ or the IPCC value of 0.35 g kg- LW d-¹. For each of these cases, the N2O emissions from the field were set to be equal as described by the IPCC or set to the Iowa representative values (Jarecki et al., 2008; Chantigny et al., 2010). Setting the CH4 emission factor equal to the IPCC value resulted in a large reduction in GWP for the DP case. Changing the allocation method in synthetic fertilizer production resulted in a significant change when using the IPCC N20 emission factor (1.0%). However, the relative change is less with the Iowa representative N,O emissions (Jarecki et al., 2008; Chantigny et al., 2010). This change was also small relative to the difference between the DP and synthetic emission factors. Changing the N2O emission factors from the Iowa representative and IPCC values made a significant difference. However, the comparison between the DP and synthetic fertilizer is not materially changed by this choice. Under all of the scenarios considered, synthetic fertilizer showed significantly lower emissions than the stored manure.

In order for manure to be competitive with synthetic fertilizer with respect to GWP, a reduction in CH4 emissions from storage would be necessary. There have been some efforts to achieve this (USEPA Agstar, 2010), but these are not common practice. Other opportunities for mitigation of the environmental impacts are reduction of NH3 emissions from the housing (Jacobson, 2011) and storage (USEPA Agstar, 2010). Nitrous oxide emissions from the field may be mitigated by adding biochar to storage units or applying it with fertilizers (Steiner et al., 2010; Taghizadeh-Toosi, 2011; Taghizadeh-Toosi et al., 2012). A better understanding of the impact of soil conditions, application timing, and rate may yield N2 emission reductions as well.

In this LCA, we compared manure with synthetic fertilizer as a nutrient source for CC and CS rotations. When manure treatment techniques are compared, the functional unit is often disposal of a fixed amount of manure. The system is then expanded to calculate credits for displaced synthetic fertilizer use. This functional unit, which allows one to compare the impact of treatment of a fixed amount of manure, was the approach used by Griffing et al. (2004) and Lopez-Ridaura et al. (2009). In both studies, the manure was assumed to displace synthetic fertilizer macronutrients on a one-to-one basis. A similar approach was

Fig. 8. Scenario analysis of global warming potential (GWP) emissions per ton of corn. AA, allocate all emissions from NH2 production to NH2; DP, deep pit; IPCC CH4 use Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change emission factor for CH4 emissions from the deep pit; S, synthetic fertilizer.
taken by Ogino et al. (2012) when comparing alternative uses of concentrated rice-washing water.

When doing an LCA of crop production and comparing synthetic fertilizers with manure, the result depends on the crop macronutrient needs. Thus, the best functional unit is crop production, which was used in this article and by Cerutti et al. (2011). This functional unit forces the LCA to handle nutrient imbalances in the same manner as farm managers. It also allows for a clear illustration of the impacts of various synthetic fertilizers in the absence of manure. In the corn fertilization case in this LCA, we assumed that all of the corn N needs would be met by N in the manure. Some excess P was assumed to build up in the soil over time. In future years, this P would eventually displace synthetic production, but no credit was applied. The amount of excess was small for DP storage and may present a significant issue with ALs. In the CS rotation, the total land area needed per unit hog was much greater (Table 6). When the N need of the crops was again supplied by manure, the P needs were not met, and we assumed that these were supplemented by synthetic fertilizer. From an LCA perspective, the underutilization of P in the absence of soybean was not an important factor. This is illustrated by the relative magnitude of impacts from P and N from synthetic fertilization (Fig. 4b). Fertilization of P has about 5 to 20% of the impact of N fertilization. In CC rotations with lagoon storage, about half of the P in swine waste was not utilized. Thus, the potential savings was about 3 to 10% of the impact from synthetic fertilizers. However, from a long-term management perspective, fertilization of a purely corn rotation with anaerobic lagoon treatment would lead to unsustainable P buildup. Because of the large emissions during housing and storage of manure, the choice of LCA boundary and the choice of whether or not to assign of a credit for alternative disposal method are important to the comparison of manure to synthetic fertilizer. In this LCA, no credit was assigned. Thus, emissions were assigned to the corn crop instead of the pork. If a credit were assigned for alternative disposal of swine manure, the environmental burdens would shift from the manured corn to the pork. When doing an LCA on animal production, it is best to include the emissions from housing and storage and use system expansion to include a credit for avoided synthetic fertilizer use. This system avoids allocation as per ISO 14044. However, it precludes a direct
comparison of the two fertilizer systems and was not suitable for this comparison.

to the fields are nearly equal to the requirements for housing, pumping, and spreading combined. However, from a global warming perspective, the impact of transport was small relative to the impact of manure CH and N2O emissions from storage and the field. In long-distance transport situations, the optimum yield economics are altered, and there may be an incentive to overapply manure. Overapplication could also lead to increased eutrophication, acidification, and GWP. Thus, the economic and ecologic optimum application rates may diverge for regions of high hog population.

For synthetic fertilizer (4b), the largest contributions to global warming and eutrophication impact were during land application (N2O emissions and NO leaching). In other categories, particularly acidification, the majority of emissions are from N fertilizer production (UAN). Triple superphosphate production contributes about 10 to 20% in many impact categories. Seventy-five percent of the fossil fuel consumption and abiotic depletion associated with TSP production was from sulfur, which is a byproduct of oil refining.

The synthetic fertilizer was compared with manure (DP and AL storage) for a CC rotation (Fig. 5). Error bars in Fig. 5 represent the 95% interval from Monte Carlo simulations. Global warming impacts for synthetic fertilization were the

Fig. Global warming potential (GWP) by chemical emission and production step for deep pit storage with 24-km long distance transport (LDT) to field. Field, field emissions; HD, housing direct emissions; HE, housing energy emissions.
lowest. When storage systems were compared, the DP system had 50% less GWP than the AL system. This comparison was due to reduction in CH emissions from the storage system and conservation of N.

The acidification and eutrophication impacts, which were strongly influenced by NH3 emissions from housing, are significantly lower for DP than for AL. The DP was assumed to have NH, emissions of 18% (15-25%), versus 37% (23-51%) for the AL. These improvements were amplified by the conservation of N. On a unit crop production basis, more live weight was required for the AL than for the DP, as shown by the live weight category. Consequently, the amount of N treated and associated acidification, eutrophication, GWP, and photochemical oxidation impacts were higher. The live weight needed was 57 + 15 kg d LW kg- corn for AL and 36 + 8 kg d LW kg-1 corn for DP. In Fig. 5, the error bars for live weight reflect relatively large uncertainties in the swine waste characterization data (N production per unit live weight) as well as the emissions of N in storage. However, variations in swine waste characteristics do not affect the comparison between storage methods. Thus, the error bars here are indicative of the certainty between manure and synthetic fertilizer and not between storage methods.

The comparison between DP, AL, and synthetic for the CS rotation is qualitatively similar to that of the CC rotation. On a unit product basis, however, the N applied in the CS rotation is much lower and contributes to lower GWP emissions. In the CC rotation, GWP emissions were 0.82, and 0.25 kg CO2e kg-1 corn for the AL, DP, and synthetic fertilizers, respectively. In the CS rotation, GWP emissions were 1.26, 0.58, and 0.19 kg CO2e (kg corn plus 0.298 kg soybean) for the AL, DP, and synthetic fertilizers, respectively. The addition of soybean lowers the net fertilization emission due to the N credit produced by fixation. This compares with an emission of 0.2 kg CO kg-1 corn in the 2012 GREET model
